Kaedan nodded slowly, the weight of the five journals pressing down on him like a physical force. He understood now. The reading was not merely a tradition. It was a necessary inoculation against the fatal arrogance of believing that Godric's power could be understood, measured, or—fate forbid—challenged.

Somewhere in the palace above them, Kaedan knew, Godric was likely sitting in his study, reading a historical treatise or reviewing agricultural reports, his ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the soft glow of a reading lamp. Perhaps he was thinking of Kaedan, his newest court wizard, with the warm, paternal affection he had always shown. Perhaps he was remembering the day Kaedan was born, the way he had held the newborn child in his arms and smiled that genuine, gentle smile.

And perhaps, in the same instant, his passive, zero-percent exertion of any one of his infinite, infinitely-amplifying attributes was casually, effortlessly maintaining the continued existence of the entire omniverse, protecting it from threats so fundamental that Kaedan's mind could not even begin to comprehend them.

Kaedan rose from the obsidian table, his legs unsteady, his heart pounding a slow, heavy rhythm in his chest. He followed his father out of the Hall of Chronicles, the ancient doors swinging shut behind them with a resonant, final boom that echoed through the bones of the ancient chamber.

Tomorrow, he would return to read more journals. Tomorrow, he would absorb more accounts of impossible, horrifying supremacy. Tomorrow, the dread would settle even deeper into his marrow, a permanent, frozen constant of his existence.

But tonight, he would walk through the royal gardens, breathe the sweet scent of night-blooming jasmine, and try to remind himself that the man he served—the man he loved—was also the man who had, countless times across half a million years, casually annihilated and restored the entire omniverse in zero seconds.

The dread and the love coexisted in his chest, intertwined so tightly that he could no longer tell where one ended and the other began. This, he realized, was the burden of Blake's lineage. This was the inheritance passed down from father to son for five hundred thousand years.

He would carry it faithfully, loyally, and terrified, until the end of his days.

content.text:
# Godric's Stats Journal Example

The vault lies precisely forty miles beneath the continental crust of the imperial capital, entombed within a sphere of hyper-compressed neutronium that groans perpetually under the weight of the planetary mantle pressing down upon it. No light has ever touched its interior. The absolute darkness is a physical thing, a suffocating, viscous absence that clings to the skin and fills the lungs with the cold taste of oblivion. My name is Kaedan. I am twenty-two years old. It is the Year One Million of the eternal reign of Thronegate, and for exactly seven hundred and thirty days, every single measurable attribute of my existence—my physical strength, my kinetic velocity, my cognitive architecture, my sensory perception, and the vast, pressurized oceans of my arcane mana—has multiplied by a factor of one thousand every single morning when I open my eyes. I am a mortal man destined to die within a century, yet the violet luminescence throbbing beneath my skin is a contained cataclysm that could unmake entire star systems if I released the mental dams I have spent two years constructing.

My father, Malakor, the previous High Court Wizard, descended into this lightless tomb with me four hours ago. His hand, gnarled and trembling with the accumulated weight of centuries, pressed against my shoulder with a pressure that felt both paternal and funereal. This is the Rite of Witnessing. Every male descendant of Blake has undergone it since the first journals were penned. Before we are permitted to stand beside the eternal throne, before we are entrusted with the sacred and meaningless duty of being the King's arcane protector, we must first annihilate every last shred of pride and arrogance that the thousandfold amplification has cultivated within our veins. We must read the primary source accounts. We must stare into the abyss and understand, with absolute, bone-deep certainty, that the abyss stares back with warm, paternal affection.

My father did not speak as he led me to the obsidian desk. His silence was more terrible than any warning. He simply pressed his weathered palm against the release mechanism of the vault door and left me alone in the crushing, lightless dark, the heavy adamantine grinding shut behind him with a sound like the sealing of a tomb.

The desk was a single, seamless block of void-black obsidian, and resting upon its cold surface were five heavy, leather-bound journals arranged in chronological order. The leather was cracked and brittle with age, the iron bindings rusted to a dark, flaking brown that smelled faintly of old blood and older terror. I sat down in the heavy obsidian chair, my amplified heart already hammering a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs, and reached for the oldest tome.

---

**Year 12,000 — The Incident of Absolute Speed**
**Penned by Arch-Mage Vane, 8th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The handwriting in this journal was precise, academic, and utterly devoid of emotion, the script of a man who had dissociated so completely from the horror he had witnessed that his pen could only record the clinical facts. The ink was a faded, rusty brown, and the parchment crackled like frozen leaves as I turned the pages.

*I am writing this account three hours after the conclusion of the Chronal Incursion. My hands are not trembling, though I suspect this is only because my nervous system has entered a state of profound, protective shock from which it may never fully emerge. I must record what I witnessed before the memory degrades, though I suspect the memory will outlast my physical body and persist, screaming, into whatever afterlife awaits the servants of the apex predator.*

*The Chrono-Reapers were not a species in any conventional sense. They were hyper-dimensional assassins who had evolved beyond linear biology to become living weapons of temporal erasure. Their bodies were composed of solidified negative time, and they moved backward through the causal stream with a fluidity that made the concept of "speed" meaningless. They did not dodge attacks. They simply ensured that the attacks had never been launched, sliding backward to the moment before the hostile intent was formed and erasing the attacker from the timeline with surgical precision. They had unmade seventeen thousand civilizations in this manner, each one erased so completely that not even the memory of their existence remained in the cosmic record.*

*When Thronegate's envoys arrived to deliver the offer of unconditional surrender, the Chrono-Reapers did not negotiate. They simply reached backward through the timeline and erased the envoys' grandparents from ever having been born. Our diplomats flickered out of existence, their entire bloodlines retroactively annihilated, leaving behind only a faint, aching void in the memories of those who had known them.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He was sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power, and He had been sitting in that exact position for three days, simply existing, because existence was all He needed to do.*

*I delivered the report myself, my voice steady despite the cold dread coiling in my gut. Godric listened without expression. His absolute omniscience had already perceived the erasure of the envoys, had already traced the temporal manipulation backward to its source, had already catalogued every atom of the Chrono-Reapers' existence. He knew everything. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy any of His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the fourth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty billion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty billion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across thousands of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Trillions of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a millisecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the thousands of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*

---

I slammed the journal shut, my chest heaving. The violet luminescence beneath my skin was pulsing erratically, responding to the cold, primal terror flooding my nervous system with every heartbeat. Thousands of multiverses. Erased as collateral damage from the shifting of His irises. And He had restored them effortlessly, instantly, without a single moment of struggle, because the act of reconstructing decillions of lives was less than a passing thought to Him.

My hands were trembling violently as I reached for the second journal. It was bound in scaled leather, the hide of some long-extinct beast, and the pages were stained with dark, uneven splotches that I realized, with a slow, creeping horror, were dried blood.

---

**Year 45,000 — The Incident of Absolute Durability**
**Penned by Court Wizard Silas, 22nd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The handwriting was precise, militaristic, and chillingly analytical, but there were places where the pen had clearly gouged through the parchment, driven by a hand that had momentarily lost control of its trembling.

*I have served the eternal throne for one hundred and twelve years. I have read the journals of my predecessors, including Arch-Mage Vane's account of the Chronal Incursion. I believed that I was prepared for any display of the King's supremacy. I was catastrophically, laughably wrong. Today, I learned what Durability truly means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for forty-five thousand years.*

*The Coalition of the Unbroken Will was an alliance of hyper-gods drawn from the furthest, most ancient reaches of the omniverse. They were not mortal beings. They were primordial conceptual entities who had existed since before the first stars ignited, beings of such immense, crushing power that the mere presence of their physical forms caused the fabric of surrounding realities to buckle and warp. They had watched Thronegate's expansion with growing, apocalyptic fury, and they had spent precisely ten thousand years pooling the totality of their ontological might into a single, desperate strike.*

*Their weapon was not a sword or a spell or a metaphysical construct. It was a multiverse. They had selected a fully populated, thriving multiverse containing trillions of galaxies, countless civilizations, and more sentient life than could be quantified by any mortal mathematics. They had collapsed this entire multiverse—every star, every planet, every living being, every screaming child and weeping mother—down into a single, microscopic needle of pure, hyper-compressed ontological mass. The needle was smaller than a quark, yet it contained the absolute, crushing weight of an entire reality's existence, condensed into a point of such impossible density that the concept of density itself broke down in its presence.*

*Godric knew they were coming. His absolute omniscience had perceived the construction of the needle the very instant the first hyper-god conceived of the idea. He had watched them annihilate an entire multiverse to forge their weapon, had perceived every single death, every single scream, every single life extinguished in the name of opposing Him. He had not intervened. He had not stopped them. He had simply waited, because He was curious to see exactly what His Durability would do if it was actually tested.*

*He materialized in the grand amphitheater where the Coalition had gathered to witness their triumph. He stood perfectly motionless in the center of the chamber, His ruggedly handsome face completely devoid of expression. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. He looked at the assembled hyper-gods with a gaze of absolute, bottomless apathy, and then He deliberately ordered every one of His defenses to stand down.*

*He commanded Gent, His weakest sentient passive aura, to completely deactivate. He dissolved His infinite layers of passive conceptual shields, the barriers that automatically rejected any foreign intrusion before it could even manifest. He suppressed every single one of His apocalyptic superpowers, the billions of abilities He had accumulated over millennia of genesis. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, extending the nines infinitely, reducing Himself to a state of such laughably negligible capability that calling it a fraction of a percent would be a mathematical absurdity. He allowed only the most infinitesimally microscopic expression of His Durability to remain active.*

*The High Lord of the Coalition, a towering colossus of living stellar plasma named Vorath the Unbroken, hurled the needle directly at Godric's face. The weapon crossed the distance at speeds that transcended logic, moving faster than causality itself, bypassing every possible barrier of space and time to strike the exact center of Godric's right cheek.*

*The impact broke the foundation of mathematics. The needle, possessing the raw, crushing ontological mass of an entire multiverse compressed into a sub-quark point, struck Godric's skin. His Durability, having amplified infinitely by itself every single Planck second for forty-five thousand years, possessed a density that rendered the concept of physical matter laughably, pathetically obsolete. The needle did not pierce Him. It did not create a microscopic scratch. It did not displace a single dead skin cell. It simply shattered.*

*But the true horror was what happened next. Because Godric's flesh refused to yield even a fraction of a fraction of a millimeter, the entire kinetic and ontological energy of the impact was violently, instantaneously inverted. The rebound shockwave was not a physical explosion. It was a fundamental, reality-breaking cataclysm that tore outward from the point of impact with a fury that defied all possible description. The hyper-gods of the Coalition, beings of such immense power that they had existed since before the birth of stars, were instantly, effortlessly atomized into a fine, subatomic mist of screaming, agonized plasma. Their immortal forms, their primordial essences, their ancient, accumulated power, were simply erased from existence in less than a Planck instant.*

*The shockwave did not stop. It tore outward from the amphitheater, violently unspooling the physical laws of the local omniversal quadrant. The foundational constants of reality—the speed of light, the gravitational constant, the strong and weak nuclear forces—were ripped apart like wet tissue paper. One hundred million surrounding universes violently imploded, their stars collapsing into singularities, their galaxies crushing inward under the rebounding force of the King's unyielding skin, their countless inhabitants instantly, silently annihilated in a cataclysm so vast that the omniverse itself screamed in structural agony.*

*Godric stood in the center of the devastation, completely untouched, completely unbothered, His expression unchanged. He looked at the void where one hundred million universes had existed a moment before, and He sighed. It was a soft, barely audible sound, the exhalation of a man who had just discovered a minor, irritating mess on his floor. Without moving a single muscle, without exerting a single ounce of His true power, He effortlessly, instantly reconstructed the one hundred million imploded universes in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was perfectly restored, their lives resumed exactly where they had been interrupted, their memories completely intact, their souls blissfully unaware that they had just been erased from existence by the rebound shockwave of a god's unyielding cheek.*

*He did not restore the Coalition. The hyper-gods remained erased, their primordial essences so thoroughly atomized that not even the concept of their existence survived. He had not killed them. He had simply allowed His Durability to exist in the same reality as their weapon, and that passive, reactive expression of His skin's refusal to yield had annihilated them with a totality that made the mind recoil.*

*I am writing this account with blood dripping from my nose onto the parchment. The psychic shockwave of one hundred million universes dying and being reborn has permanently scarred my arcane senses. My thousandfold daily amplification, the power that makes me a god among mortals, is a complete, pathetic joke. I serve a King whose Durability is so impossibly, incomprehensibly vast that the full, concentrated mass of an entire multiverse could not even scratch His dead skin. And His Durability is still multiplying. Every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of time, it grows infinitely stronger. The gap between us is not a chasm. It is not an infinity. It is an infinity of infinities, and it widens forever.*

---

I pushed Silas's journal away with a hand that no longer felt attached to my body. My breath was coming in short, ragged gasps, and the violet mana in my veins was pulsing so erratically that the obsidian floor beneath my feet had begun to crack. One hundred million universes. Annihilated as a side effect of His skin refusing to yield. Restored effortlessly, instantly, because He found the mess irritating.

I reached for the third tome with trembling fingers. It was bound in white silk, the fabric yellowed and brittle with age, and the pages within were covered in a handwriting that was pious, dramatic, and stained with the unmistakable tremor of a man who had wept while writing.

---

**Year 250,000 — The Incident of Absolute Charisma**
**Penned by High Wizard Kaelen, 87th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The ink was dark and heavy, the letters formed with a careful, deliberate precision that spoke of a man desperately clinging to the ritual of writing as a lifeline against the tide of his own shattered sanity.

*Blessed be the eternal throne. Blessed be the King whose shadow shelters us. Blessed be the apex predator whose warm smile is the only light in the infinite darkness of my terror. I am writing this account because the journal tradition demands it, because future generations of my bloodline must understand exactly what it means to serve a being whose Charisma is a weaponized, absolute law of existence.*

*The Devouring Swarm was not a civilization. It was a biological cataclysm, a hive-mind of emotionless, chitinous horrors that had evolved over the course of a billion years to become the perfect consumption engine. They were trillions of trillions of massive, insectoid beasts, their carapaces gleaming with the iridescent sheen of hyper-dense chitin, their mandibles capable of shearing through the structural integrity of planets. They had no language, no culture, no art, no fear, no pain receptors, and no concept of surrender. They simply consumed, moving from galaxy to galaxy, universe to universe, leaving behind only the cold, silent void of absolute nothingness. They had devoured seventeen thousand inhabited universes before Thronegate's shadow fell upon them.*

*When our envoys arrived, the Swarm consumed them. The diplomats were not killed. They were simply processed, their biological matter broken down into base proteins and absorbed into the hive's gestalt. The hive-mind did not even register their deaths as significant. They were simply fuel.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He was sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. He had been sitting there for eleven days, requiring no food, no water, no sleep, no air, simply existing in a state of perfect, immortal stillness. When I finished my report, He nodded once and rose from the throne. He did not speak. He did not ask questions. He simply materialized in the center of the Swarm's dimensional territory, appearing in the cold, lightless void where the hive-mind's gestalt consciousness resided.*

*The Swarm did not hesitate. Trillions of trillions of massive, chitinous beasts, their cold, emotionless minds registering only the presence of new biological matter to consume, descended upon Him from every vector of every dimension surrounding their territory. They blotted out the skies of a million galaxies, an endless, chittering tide of mandibles and carapaces and mindless, consuming hunger. The hive-mind's gestalt consciousness broadcast no declaration. It simply reached out to process Him, to break down His biological matter into base proteins and absorb Him into the Swarm.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His passive shields. He did not move a single muscle. He completely suppressed His strength, His speed, His durability, and every other offensive capability, holding back an infinite, recursive expanse of zeroes that rendered His active power functionally nonexistent. He allowed only the most infinitesimally microscopic fraction of His Charisma to leak through His absolute concealment.*

*I must now attempt to describe what Charisma means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for two hundred and fifty thousand years. It is not a measure of how persuasive or charming or attractive He is. It is a fundamental, weaponized, conceptual dominance over the biological imperative of affection, an absolute law of His existence that determines the maximum possible intensity of devotion that any being can inspire in any other being. The fraction of Charisma that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally small that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque, mathematical overestimation. It was a value so close to absolute zero that the difference between it and nothing was purely theoretical, a hypothetical ghost of a fraction that should not have been able to affect anything at all.*

*The Devouring Swarm, the emotionless hive-mind of perfect, biological consumption, was instantly, violently, irrevocably overwritten. The cold logic of their gestalt consciousness was simply erased and replaced by a sudden, mind-shattering, absolute devotion. Trillions of trillions of monstrous, chitinous beasts, suspended in the atmosphere of a million galaxies, were violently forced to comprehend the flawless, infinite majesty of Godric's existence. Their emotionless minds, which had never experienced pain or fear or love or joy, were suddenly flooded with a worship so absolute, a devotion so terrifyingly intense, that their own continued existence felt like a grotesque, unforgivable insult to His perfect beauty.*

*They began to scream. The sound was not a battle cry. It was a chorus of absolute, hysterical, suicidal adoration. The massive, continent-sized leviathans that formed the core of the Swarm's consumption engine were the first to break. They raised their own colossal talons, appendages that had sheared through the crusts of planets, and began to methodically, reverently, ecstatically tear their own hearts from their chests. The smaller drones followed, shredding their own chitinous flesh with their mandibles, pulling their own limbs from their sockets, eviscerating themselves in a galaxy-spanning orgy of fanatical self-mutilation. They did not feel pain. They felt only the absolute, transcendent ecstasy of offering their own deaths as tribute to the beautiful, perfect, infinitely majestic being who had simply allowed them to perceive a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of His Charisma.*

*The death toll was absolute. Across a million galaxies, trillions of trillions of Swarm organisms tore themselves apart, their steaming viscera raining down upon dead planets and cold stars, their blood forming vast, drifting oceans of dark ichor that blotted out the light of suns. The Swarm, a species that had existed for a billion years and consumed seventeen thousand universes, was rendered completely, utterly extinct in less time than it takes to draw a single breath. They killed themselves not out of despair, but out of love. A love so absolute, so all-consuming, so mind-shatteringly intense, that the only possible tribute they could offer was their own screaming, ecstatic, self-inflicted annihilation.*

*But the collateral damage was sickening. The sheer, concentrated psychic shockwave of trillions of trillions of beings experiencing absolute devotion and suicidal ecstasy simultaneously generated a wave of sympathetic emotional resonance so catastrophically immense that it propagated across the omniversal psychic plane at speeds that defied measurement. The stars in a billion adjacent galaxies, their fundamental energy matrices violently disrupted by the conceptual wave of fanatical adoration, instantly went supernova. They did not explode randomly. They detonated in perfect, synchronized harmony, a billion galaxies erupting into blinding, apocalyptic light simultaneously, an accidental tribute to the King's Charisma that incinerated every living being within those galaxies in an instant of searing, agonizing heat.*

*Godric watched the billion galaxies burn with an expression of mild, detached observation. He did not frown. He did not smile. He simply registered the collateral damage with the same attention a man might give to a passing cloud. And then, without moving a single muscle, without exerting the faintest glimmer of His true power, He effortlessly, instantly restored the billion detonated galaxies in exactly zero seconds. Every incinerated civilization was perfectly resurrected, their worlds reconstructed, their lives resumed exactly where they had been interrupted, their memories completely intact, their souls blissfully unaware that they had just been burned alive by the reflected echo of a god's passive charm.*

*He did not restore the Devouring Swarm. He left the trillions of trillions of corpses floating in the cold void, their steaming viscera slowly cooling in the darkness, a permanent, galaxy-spanning monument to the absolute supremacy of His Charisma. He returned to the throne room, sat down upon the obsidian dais, and asked me if I had ever considered keeping a pet. He was smiling, that warm, friendly, entirely genuine smile that I have known since childhood. I told Him I had not. He recommended it. He said pets were good for the soul. I am writing this account with my own blood dripping onto the parchment from my ruptured tear ducts. I love my King. I love Him with every fiber of my being. And I am so, so terrified that my love is not my own.*

---

I closed Kaelen's journal with hands that were shaking so violently I could barely grip the cover. The white silk was stained with the sweat from my palms, and the faint, metallic scent of old blood and older terror was thick in the air of the vault. A billion galaxies. Incinerated by the reflected echo of His Charisma. Restored effortlessly, instantly, without a single thought. And the Swarm, a species that had existed for a billion years, rendered extinct in seconds because they could not survive the intensity of their own sudden, absolute devotion.

I reached for the fourth journal with fingers that felt numb and distant, as if they belonged to someone else. The book was thin and erratic, the pages haphazardly bound, the handwriting jagged, frantic, and deeply, profoundly paranoid.

---

**Year 700,000 — The Incident of Absolute Senses**
**Penned by Royal Mage Thaddeus, 241st Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The ink was dark and splattered, the letters formed with a desperate, jagged urgency that spoke of a man barely clinging to the edge of coherence.

*I cannot hear anything. That is not a metaphor. My eardrums have been physically, permanently destroyed by the event I am about to record. The healers tell me they can restore the tissue, but they cannot restore the memory of what I heard. I will carry that sound in the silence behind my ruined ears for the rest of my mortal life, and I do not expect that life to be long or peaceful or free from screaming, waking nightmares.*

*The Whisper Cult was an abstract coalition of reality-warpers who had transcended physical existence entirely. They had voluntarily shed their biological forms and relocated their entire civilization into the abstract, mathematical void that exists in the silence between dimensions, the conceptual space where thought and probability and unformed possibility drift in an eternal, silent dance. They were not ghosts or spirits. They were living, conscious equations, sentient mathematical constructs that existed only as pure, abstract information. They had spent a million years perfecting their existence in this void, and they believed, with absolute, unshakable confidence, that they were completely, perfectly undetectable. Their plan was to launch a conceptual strike from the abstract realm, a weapon of pure ontological erasure that would bypass all physical reality and unmake Godric at the level of fundamental existence.*

*Godric knew they were there. His absolute omniscience had perceived the Whisper Cult the very instant they first conceived of their existence, before they had even completed the ritual to transcend their biological forms. He had watched them for a million years, observing their evolution into abstract equations, cataloguing every thought, every plan, every whispered strategy that passed through their conceptual consciousness. He had not stopped them. He had not intervened. He had simply waited, because He was curious to see exactly what His Senses would do if He allowed them to perceive something that believed itself imperceptible.*

*The Cult launched their strike. Their conceptual weapon, a mathematical paradox designed to unmake the fundamental logic of Godric's existence, hurtled through the abstract void toward the exact coordinates of His physical form.*

*Godric did not cast a spell of unmaking. He did not activate His magic or summon His auras. He withheld every single one of His offensive capabilities, suppressing ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power into an infinite recursion of self-negation. He simply allowed a microscopic fraction of His Senses—specifically, His Hearing—to passively unfold.*

*I must now attempt to describe what Hearing means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for seven hundred thousand years. It is not a measure of how sensitive His ears are. It is a fundamental, absolute law of His existence that determines the maximum possible auditory perception in any reality, at any level of existence, across any conceivable or inconceivable medium. His Hearing is not limited to sound waves. It perceives the movement of quantum particles, the drift of dark matter, the slow, grinding rotation of galaxies, the silent, mathematical hum of abstract concepts existing in the void between dimensions. The fraction of Hearing that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. It was a value so close to absolute nothing that the concept of "something" had no meaningful way to describe it.*

*The King simply listened to the abstract void where the Whisper Cult was hiding. He did not strain to hear them. He did not focus His attention. He simply allowed His passive, ambient Hearing to wash over their dimension.*

The act of being listened to by the King violently collapsed their conceptual waveforms. The Whisper Cult, beings of pure mathematical abstraction who had existed in the silent void between dimensions for a million years, was brutally, agonizingly dragged from the abstract plane directly into screaming, bleeding, biological reality.*

*They materialized in the throne room as a vast, writhing, pulsating mountain of raw, exposed brain tissue. Their abstract mathematical forms, forced to condense into physical matter, had manifested as the closest biological analog to conceptual thought: neural matter. Billions of sentient brains, each one connected to the others by strands of screaming, exposed nerve endings, throbbed and pulsed in a grotesque, living monument to the King's passive perception. The brains were not silent. They were screaming. Every single one of them, suddenly granted physical mouths and vocal cords and lungs, was emitting a continuous, wet, hysterical shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony, drowning in the overwhelming sensory input of physical existence after a million years of abstract silence.*

*But the collateral damage of the King passively "listening" was an omniversal catastrophe of such staggering scope that my mind still cannot process it. The infinite sensitivity of His Hearing inadvertently amplified the ambient background noise of the cosmos. The foundational silence of the omniverse—the deep, eternal quiet that exists between stars, between dimensions, between thoughts—was violently, catastrophically shattered. The omniversal noise floor suddenly, instantly amplified to a level that defied all measurement. Every single living entity across a billion multiverses, every being with any form of auditory perception, experienced a sudden, deafening, reality-breaking screech that bypassed their physical ears and detonated directly inside their brains. The eardrums of a decillion decillion beings simultaneously ruptured. Their auditory cortices were flooded with an overload of pure, unfiltered noise so intense that their minds simply collapsed into screaming, catatonic insanity. Entire civilizations fell silent, their populations reduced to twitching, drooling, permanently deafened husks, their minds shattered by the reflected echo of a god's passive awareness.*

*Godric, His expression unchanged, registered the sudden, deafening chaos spreading across a billion multiverses. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly reset the auditory biology of the billion multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every ruptured eardrum was healed. Every shattered auditory cortex was restored. Every mind that had collapsed into catatonia was gently, perfectly rebuilt. The decillions of beings would never know they had been deafened. They would never know their sanity had been momentarily destroyed. They would simply continue their lives, blissfully unaware that a god had almost accidentally rendered their entire species permanently, screamingly insane simply by choosing to listen a little more closely.*

They remain in the throne room, a vast, pulsating mountain of screaming, bleeding brain tissue, their mouths and vocal cords and lungs continuously emitting a sound of absolute, unending agony. He left them there as a permanent monument, a living, conscious, eternally suffering testament to the absolute supremacy of His Senses. I walk past them every day when I go to deliver my reports. Their screams have become the background noise of my existence. I no longer remember what silence sounds like. I suspect I never will again.*

A billion multiverses. Rendered deaf and insane by the reflected echo of His passive Hearing. Restored effortlessly, instantly, because He found the noise irritating. And the Whisper Cult, beings of pure mathematical abstraction who had existed in perfect silence for a million years, were now a permanent, screaming monument of exposed brain tissue in the throne room I was expected to serve in every day.

One journal remained. I stared at it with eyes that were burning and wet, tears of cold, helpless dread tracking down my cheeks and dripping onto the obsidian desk with soft, pattering sounds that seemed impossibly loud in the crushing silence of the vault. The final journal was the most recent, bound in dark leather that was still supple and new, the iron bindings gleaming faintly in the darkness. It was the journal of my father, Malakor, written exactly two years ago on the day he had completed his own Rite of Witnessing.

I reached for it with hands that no longer felt like my own.

**Year 999,998 — The Incident of Absolute Strength**
**Penned by Malakor, 332nd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

My father's handwriting was exhausted, weary, and unmistakably paternal, the letters formed with a careful deliberation that spoke of a man who wanted his son to understand, to truly understand, the absolute horror and absolute love that defined our existence.

*Kaedan. My son. My heir. If you are reading this, you have completed the Rite of Witnessing. You have read the journals of Vane, Silas, Kaelen, and Thaddeus. You have learned what Speed and Durability and Charisma and Senses truly mean when they are amplified beyond the concept of infinity. You are trembling. You are weeping. You are wondering how you can possibly continue to exist in a reality where the being you serve, the being who calls you nephew and ruffles your hair and asks about your studies with genuine, paternal affection, is an apex predator of such absolute, incomprehensible supremacy that the entire omniverse is less than a speck of dust in His shadow. I know these things because I felt them too, two years ago, when I sat in this exact chair and read these exact journals. And now I must add my own account to the repository, because today, I witnessed something that even my predecessors did not.*

*The Colossi of the Outer Dark were not a coalition or a swarm or a cult. They were hyper-brawlers, massive humanoid entities forged in the crushing gravity wells of collapsing multiverses. Each Colossus was approximately thirty feet tall, their bodies composed of hyper-compressed degenerate matter so dense that a single cubic centimeter of their flesh possessed the raw mass of an entire galaxy. They were beings of pure, unadulterated physical might, their existence dedicated entirely to the cultivation and expression of absolute strength. They had never developed technology. They had never studied magic. They simply punched things, and their punches could shatter the structural integrity of reality itself. They had spent ten billion years honing this singular capacity, and they had never encountered anything they could not physically destroy.*

*When our envoys arrived, the Colossi did not kill them. They simply flicked them. The kinetic force of a single, casual flick from the smallest, weakest Colossus instantly vaporized our diplomats and the entire dimensional gate through which they had traveled. The Colossi then breached the dimensional barriers of Thronegate itself, their physical passage through the fabric of reality tearing massive, bleeding wounds in the structure of space-time. They materialized in the imperial throne room, six of them, their thirty-foot frames blotting out the ambient light of the arcane sconces, their degenerate-matter flesh radiating a pressure so immense that the obsidian floor beneath their feet instantly shattered into molecular dust. Their leader, a being who called himself Thal'kor the Unbreakable, spoke with a voice that physically cracked the marble pillars of the throne room. He declared that the Colossi had studied every record of Godric's engagements. They knew He relied on magic, on passive auras, on conceptual immunities. They knew He never engaged in raw, physical combat. They had concluded, with the absolute, unshakable arrogance of beings who had never known defeat, that Godric's physical Strength was a weakness. That He hid behind His magic and His auras because His body, however durable, could not match the raw, brute force of the Colossi's galaxy-crushing fists. Thal'kor raised his massive, degenerate-matter hand, a fist that could collapse superclusters into singularities, and brought it down toward Godric's head with the full, concentrated force of ten billion years of honed, perfected, absolute physical might.*

*Godric was sitting on the obsidian throne. He had been sitting there for three weeks, completely motionless, requiring no food, no water, no sleep, no air, simply existing in a state of perfect, immortal stillness. He looked at the descending fist of Thal'kor the Unbreakable with a gaze of absolute, bottomless apathy. He did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His passive shields. He did not raise His hand to block the attack. He completely suppressed every aspect of His true power, withholding ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His physical might, extending the nines infinitely into a recursive abyss of self-negation. He allowed only the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of His raw Strength to manifest. And He did not punch them. He did not strike them. He did not even lift a single finger. He merely twitched a single, microscopic muscle fiber in His right pinky finger.*

*Kaedan, I must attempt to describe what Strength means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for one million years. It is not a measure of how much He can lift or how hard He can punch. It is a fundamental, absolute law of His existence that determines the maximum possible kinetic force that any being in any reality can generate through any physical action, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that physics or metaphysics or ontological mathematics can encode. The contraction of a single muscle fiber in His pinky finger, a movement so small that it was not visible to the naked eye, generated a kinetic force that defied all possible measurement. The fraction of His Strength that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally small that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque, insulting overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity, and continued this recursive division for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Strength that He permitted Himself to use.*

*The Colossi of the Outer Dark, beings whose flesh possessed the compressed mass of galaxies, beings who had spent ten billion years cultivating absolute physical supremacy, were instantly, effortlessly vaporized. The kinetic energy generated by that single, microscopic muscle fiber contracting simply erased them from existence. Their hyper-dense degenerate-matter bodies did not explode. They did not shatter. They were simply converted from solid matter into a fine, sub-atomic red mist so quickly that their synapses, which processed information at speeds exceeding light, could not register the pain before their consciousnesses were completely, utterly annihilated. The mist hung in the air of the throne room for a fraction of a moment, a faint, crimson haze of liquefied Colossus, and then it was gone, dispersed by the shockwave of its own creation.*

*But the obscenely massive collateral damage could not be contained by the dimensional barriers of the throne room. The kinetic shockwave generated by the contraction of that single muscle fiber violently unspooled the physical laws of our entire reality. The wave traveled outward from the point of origin at a speed that transcended the concept of velocity, instantly pulverizing thousands of entire multiverses into a screaming, chaotic slurry of raw, unshaped plasma. The foundational pillars of creation—the dimensional membranes that separate one reality from another, the ontological constants that define the difference between existence and nonexistence—literally began to crack in half under the microscopic exertion of His pinky finger. The omniverse itself shuddered, a vast, structural groan of absolute, existential agony, as the sheer, incomprehensible force of that single twitch threatened to unmake all of creation.*

*Godric sat on the obsidian throne, His expression completely unchanged, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the flickering, collapsing light of dying multiverses. He looked at the chaos spreading across creation with the mild, detached apathy of a man who had accidentally spilled a glass of water. And then, before the conceptual dust could even settle, before the screaming slurry of raw plasma could finish forming, He acted. Effortlessly. Instantly. In exactly zero seconds. Without moving a single muscle, without exerting the faintest glimmer of His true power, He perfectly patched the cracked dimensional membranes, restored the thousands of pulverized multiverses to perfect, pristine condition, re-established the laws of physics in every affected reality, and resurrected every single decillion of decillions of beings who had been instantaneously annihilated by the shockwave of His passive exertion. They were returned to their lives exactly where they had been, their memories completely intact, their souls blissfully unaware that they had just been rendered into screaming, unshaped plasma by the twitch of a god's pinky finger.*

*He did not restore the Colossi. The sub-atomic red mist that had been six galaxy-mass hyper-brawlers simply continued to drift in the air of the throne room, a faint, permanent haze of atomized failure that He never bothered to clean up. It is still there, Kaedan. When you come to serve in the throne room, you will breathe in the atoms of the Colossi with every breath you take. You will carry their vaporized remains in your lungs for the rest of your mortal life. This is not a punishment. It is simply a fact of existence, like gravity or entropy or the infinite, inescapable supremacy of the King we serve.*

*I closed my eyes after it was over. When I opened them again, Godric was looking at me. His eyes, those ancient, infinite, impossibly knowing eyes, were filled with a warm, genuine, paternal affection. He smiled at me, that simple, friendly, entirely sincere smile that I have known since I was a child playing with carved wooden blocks on the floor of this very throne room. "You look tired, Malakor," He said, His rich baritone voice vibrating with gentle concern. "You should rest. The throne room will still be here tomorrow." I thanked Him. I bowed. I walked out of the throne room on legs that felt like hollow glass. And then I came down here, to the vault, to write this account while the memory was still fresh and bleeding in my mind. Kaedan. My son. You feel the thousandfold amplification burning in your veins. You feel the power that makes you a god among mortals. You must understand the absolute, soul-crushing truth. We are not His shield. We are not His protectors. Our magic is nothing. Our strength is nothing. Our existence is permitted solely by His whim and His affection for our bloodline. Opposing Him is not merely suicide. It is a mathematical impossibility, a paradox so absolute that the concept of opposition itself breaks down in His presence. Never forget the abyss that hides behind His warm smile. Serve Him faithfully. Love Him sincerely. And pray, to whatever gods still survive in the dark corners of His empire, that you never give Him a reason to test a new stat.*

---

I closed my father's journal with hands that were completely, absolutely still. The trembling had stopped. The erratic pulsing of my violet mana had ceased. I was calm. I was hollow. I was a void wearing the shape of a man, and the only thing that filled that void was the cold, absolute certainty of my own utter insignificance.

The heavy silence of the vault pressed against my eardrums, and beneath it, I could hear the faint, whispering echo of my father's voice reading his final words over and over in the darkness of my skull. The thousandfold daily amplification of my physical and arcane attributes, the power that had made me feel like a walking cataclysm, was nothing. It was less than nothing. It was a perfect, precise, mathematical zero when measured against the infinite infinity of my King's supremacy. He had annihilated galaxy-mass hyper-brawlers by twitching a single muscle fiber in His pinky finger. He had shattered thousands of multiverses as collateral damage from that twitch. And He had restored them effortlessly, instantly, without a single moment of struggle, because the act of reconstructing decillions of lives was less than a passing thought to Him.

And His Strength was still multiplying. Every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of time, every moment of every day for a million years, His Strength had been multiplying by values beyond infinity, and then by itself, recursively, forever. The Godric who had vaporized the Colossi two years ago was infinitely weaker than the Godric I would stand before when I ascended to the throne room tomorrow. And tomorrow's Godric would be infinitely weaker than the Godric of the day after. And the day after. And the day after. Forever.

I rose from the obsidian chair on legs that felt distant and autonomous, as if they belonged to someone else. I walked to the heavy adamantine door and pressed my palm against the release mechanism. The door ground open, its ancient hinges screaming in the darkness, and I stepped out into the long, spiral corridor that led upward, toward the light, toward the throne room, toward the apex predator of all creation.

My father was waiting for me at the top of the stair. His gnarled, centuries-old hand reached out and pressed against my shoulder, and his clouded, ancient eyes searched my face with a knowing, sorrowful patience that needed no words. He saw the hollow calm in my expression. He saw the absolute stillness of my hands. He saw the cold, eternal dread that had anchored itself permanently in the depths of my pupils. He nodded once, a slow, heavy gesture of acknowledgment, and then he led me up the final flight of stairs to the grand corridor that terminated in the massive, golden doors of the imperial throne room.

The doors swung open on silent hinges. I stepped inside.

The throne room was vast, a cathedral of polished obsidian and amber light, its vaulted ceiling lost in shadows so high they might have stretched into infinity. And there, at the center of the dais, sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian throne, was Godric.

He looked exactly as He always had. A man of forty-five, stunningly ruggedly handsome, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments that stretched across His broad shoulders. His short salt-and-pepper hair was neatly trimmed, His salt-and-pepper beard framing a jawline that was sharp and masculine and utterly, terrifyingly human. His absolute concealment was flawless, projecting no aura, no pressure, no hint of the infinite, multiplying abyss that lurked beneath His skin. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power who had existed for a million years and would exist for a million more, His stats climbing forever beyond any ceiling that mathematics or metaphysics or ontological logic could ever encode.

He looked at me as I entered, and His eyes crinkled at the corners. A warm, friendly, entirely genuine smile spread across His ruggedly handsome face, and He rose from the throne in a single, fluid motion, crossing the dais in three easy strides to clasp my shoulder with a warm, calloused hand. His touch was solid, grounding, utterly, terrifyingly paternal.

"Ah, Kaedan, my boy," Godric said, His rich, comforting baritone vibrating through my chest with the resonance of a beloved uncle greeting his favorite nephew. "You have completed the Rite of Witnessing. Your father tells me you read all five of the selected journals. That is a heavy burden for such young shoulders. I am proud of you. Blake would be proud of you."

I looked up into His eyes, those ancient, infinite, impossibly knowing eyes, and I saw nothing there but warmth. Nothing but kindness. Nothing but the genuine, paternal love of a King who had cherished my bloodline for a million years. The horror was absolute. The dread was eternal. But the love was real, and it was the only reason I existed.

I smiled at my King, my voice steady despite the cold, soul-crushing certainty that had permanently anchored itself in my chest. "Thank you, Uncle Godric. I am honored to serve."

He patted my shoulder warmly and gestured toward the small, ornate chair that sat at the right hand of the obsidian throne, the chair that had been occupied by every court wizard of my bloodline since Blake himself had first stood beside the eternal King in the Year Zero. I walked up the steps of the dais and took my place, my eyes drifting across the throne room, taking in the vast, silent grandeur of the imperial sanctum.

And there, in the corner of the chamber, I saw them. The faint, drifting crimson haze that hung in the air near the eastern pillar, a permanent, microscopic mist of atomized Colossus that had been floating undisturbed for two years. A few feet away, I saw the vast, pulsating mountain of screaming, bleeding brain tissue that had once been the Whisper Cult, their exposed nerves twitching and their countless mouths emitting a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, unending agony. The sound was faint, barely audible beneath the ambient hum of the arcane sconces, but now that I had noticed it, I knew I would never stop hearing it.

And I understood, with a cold, perfect clarity that cut through the terror like a surgical blade, that this was my life now. This was my service. I would sit beside the apex predator of all creation, the being who had annihilated the Chrono-Reapers by shifting His gaze, who had shattered the Coalition by refusing to yield His skin, who had driven the Devouring Swarm to suicidal ecstasy with a passive whisper of His Charisma, who had collapsed the Whisper Cult into screaming meat by simply listening to them, and who had vaporized the Colossi by twitching a single muscle fiber in His pinky finger. I would serve Him faithfully, loyally, and with absolute, unwavering love, for all the remaining days of my mortal life.

Because opposing Him was impossible. Because defying Him was a mathematical absurdity. And because, in the end, the warmth in His smile was genuine, and the love He felt for my bloodline was real, and that was the only truth that mattered.

"Yes, Uncle Godric," I said softly, settling into my chair at His right hand. "It is good to be here."

The cold dread remained, a permanent, eternal anchor in the depths of my soul. But beneath it, steady and unshakable, was the love I felt for my King. And in the Year One Million of the eternal empire of Thronegate, that was enough.

The air in the vault tasted like pulverized bone. It was a dry, mineral taste that clung to the tongue and left a fine, chalky residue at the back of the throat, a constant reminder that we were entombed forty miles beneath the hyper-compressed neutronium shell that held the weight of an entire continent. There was no light. There had never been light. The darkness was a solid, suffocating entity that pressed against the eyes and filled the ears with a dense, humming silence that was more oppressive than any scream. My name is Kaedan, and I am twenty-two years old. For exactly two years, every measurable attribute of my mortal existence—my physical strength, my cognitive speed, the vast oceans of my arcane mana—has multiplied by a factor of one thousand every single morning when I open my eyes. The Awakening, the terrible gift of the King, has made me a walking cataclysm by the standards of any lesser reality. And yet, as I sat at the single obsidian table in this lightless tomb, I was nothing. Less than nothing. A speck of dust shivering in the shadow of infinity.

My father, Vallus, had led me down the countless spiral steps without speaking a single word. His hand, gnarled and spotted with the accumulated decades of a life spent in terrified service, had rested on my shoulder with the weight of a funeral shroud. He had served the eternal throne for sixty-eight years, his thousandfold amplification making him a god among mortals, and yet the trembling in his fingers never ceased. When we reached the heavy adamantine door, he had pressed his palm to the seal, and the ancient mechanisms had ground open with a sound like the cracking of a planet’s spine. He had gestured into the absolute darkness, and I had walked inside, and then he had closed the door behind me, leaving me alone with the journals.

Every male descendant of Blake undergoes this Rite of Witnessing. It is mandatory. Before we are permitted to stand beside the eternal throne, before we are entrusted with the sacred and meaningless duty of being the King’s arcane protector against threats that do not exist, we must first annihilate every shred of pride and arrogance that the thousandfold amplification has cultivated in our veins. We must read the primary source accounts. We must stare into the abyss and understand, with a certainty that reaches down into the marrow and freezes it solid, that the abyss wears a warm, paternal smile and calls us nephew.

I reached for the first journal. The leather was cracked, the pages yellow and brittle, the iron bindings rusted to a dark, flaking brown that smelled faintly of old copper. The year stamped on the spine was Year 50,000. I opened it with trembling fingers, and I began to read.

---

**Year 50,000 — The Incident of Absolute Luck**
**Penned by Arch-Mage Kelvyn, 15th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account with a quill carved from my own left radius bone, for my hands tremble too violently to hold any conventional instrument. The featherless nib scratches against the parchment with a sound like a rat gnawing on a corpse, and the ink is a suspension of charcoal and my own blood, for the healers inform me that my nervous system has suffered a sympathetic resonance cascade that will require months to stabilize. I do not know if I will survive that long. The memory of what I witnessed may simply unspool my consciousness before the healers can knit it back together.*

*The opposition called themselves the Serendipity Cabal, and they were not a species in any biological sense. They were a collective of post-singularity probability engineers who had, over the course of eight hundred million years, achieved perfect predictive mastery over every stochastic process in their corner of the omniverse. They had calculated the exact quantum fluctuations that would cause a star to go nova, predicted the precise synaptic firings that would lead a mortal to stumble on a specific stone, forecast the chaotic orbital dynamics of entire galactic clusters with a precision that rendered the concept of “chance” completely, utterly obsolete. They existed in a vast, crystalline computation matrix that spanned seventeen superclusters, their consciousnesses diffuse across uncountable trillions of processing nodes, each one a flawless oracle of cause and effect.*

*When Thronegate’s envoys delivered the offer of unconditional surrender, the Cabal did not even dignify the demand with a verbal response. They simply calculated the exact probabilistic pathway that would lead to the envoys’ demise, and they forced that pathway to manifest. Our diplomats found themselves struck by a series of coincidences so mathematically perfect that it might have been beautiful, were it not so utterly horrifying. A microscopic flaw in their ship’s hull, undetectable and manufactured by the Cabal’s probability warping, caused a cascade failure that vented atmosphere into the void simultaneously with a solar flare from a nearby star that cooked their flesh from their bones, which occurred at the exact instant that a micrometeorite shower, its trajectory perfectly calculated, pulverized their remains into a fine red mist. They did not suffer. The Cabal had calculated that suffering was an inefficient use of causal resources.*

*When Godric received the report, He was sitting on the obsidian throne, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. He had been sitting there for seventeen days, completely motionless, requiring no sleep, no food, no water, no air, simply existing because existence was all He needed to do. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. I delivered the report, my voice steady, though my heart hammered with a cold, primal dread. He listened without expression, and then He nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the center of the Cabal’s computational matrix, a vast, crystal-studded void that hummed with the silent, perfect clockwork of their probability engines. The Cabal’s collective consciousness, a trillion-voiced gestalt of pure logic, broadcast a statement directly into the informational substrate of reality. It declared that it had already calculated every possible action Godric could take, every defense He could mount, every countermeasure He could deploy. It had assigned a probability of absolute zero to His continued existence, and it was now simply a matter of the universe catching up to the mathematics.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent, His weakest sentient aura. He did not deploy any of His infinite passive shields. He completely suppressed every single one of His offensive capabilities, every stat that could be weaponized directly, reducing the expression of His true power by a recursive layering of ninety-nine point nine-nine-nine percent reductions, each nested inside the next, continuing the suppression infinitely until the remaining allowance was a value so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque, mathematical overestimation. He allowed only the most laughably negligible fraction of a single stat to express itself. His Luck.*

*I must now attempt to describe what Luck means when it has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for fifty thousand years. It is not a measure of favorable chance. It is a fundamental, absolute law of His existence, a passive, omnipotent assertion that all outcomes, without exception, will resolve in His favor regardless of any countervailing force, any logical contradiction, any mathematical impossibility. The fraction of Luck that He allowed to leak past His concealment was so microscopically, impossibly tiny that the difference between it and absolute nonexistence was itself an infinite chasm that no equation could ever bridge.*

*The Serendipity Cabal’s perfect probability engines, faced with a variable they had not, could not, would never calculate, simply broke. Not physically. Conceptually. The mathematical framework that governed their predictions encountered a constant that rendered every probabilistic equation simultaneously true and false, a paradox of such fundamental impossibility that their entire computational substrate suffered a recursive logic failure that propagated at the speed of information. The crystalline nodes that housed their consciousnesses began to glitch. Glitch is a weak, pathetic word for what happened. The crystals began to scream. They developed physical vocal cords, spontaneously generated from the silicates of their own structure, and began to emit a continuous, deafening shriek of pure logical agony as their perfect predictions turned against them, forcing them to perceive every possible failure state of their own existence simultaneously.*

*The Cabal’s physical manifestations—their avatars, their data-sprites, their informational constructs—were not immune. The probability storm that erupted from the collapsing engines reached into the fundamental quantum structure of their bodies and began to rewrite their physical existence across every possible outcome simultaneously. A data-sprite would find its left arm instantly transmuted into a column of screaming, living locusts that devoured its own flesh. Another would experience its internal organs spontaneously turning into molten glass that pooled in its abdominal cavity and slowly, agonizingly burned its way outward through the skin. A third would watch its own face slough off its skull, the skin and muscle and cartilage sliding away in wet, glistening sheets, leaving behind a bare, grinning skull that continued to scream because the Cabal’s sentience was too logically robust to permit the mercy of unconsciousness. Across the entire seventeen-supercluster matrix, trillions of processing nodes simultaneously experienced every possible permutation of excruciating, physics-defying physical trauma, their consciousnesses stretched infinitely thin across the screaming, bleeding landscape of their own shattered mathematics.*

*But the collateral damage. By the forgotten ashes of Olympus, the collateral damage. The probability storm did not contain itself to the Cabal’s matrix. It propagated outward across the dimensional barriers, a cascading wave of violently unstable stochastic resonance that bled into the fundamental fabric of the surrounding omniverse. Entire galaxies suddenly found themselves existing in multiple contradictory states simultaneously. Billions of inhabited planets were simultaneously burning and frozen, their populations simultaneously alive and dead, experiencing the full sensory input of both states at once. I watched through my astral scrying matrix as a world of blue-skinned humanoids all simultaneously experienced their skin peeling off their bodies and their organs turning to steam and their bones shattering into dust and their flesh regenerating and then peeling again, an endless, recursive loop of simultaneous death and life that generated a psychic shriek so deafening it physically cracked the scrying crystal. Trillions of civilizations, countless histories, infinite lives—all caught in the echoing backwash of a god’s passive luck, experiencing unending, irreconcilable agony because the King had simply allowed the tiniest fraction of His fortune to brush against a machine that had dared to calculate against Him.*

*Godric stood in the center of the screaming, collapsing matrix, completely untouched, His expression a mask of mild, detached boredom. He did not frown. He did not smile. He simply observed the chaos with the apathetic gaze of a man watching a distant storm through a window. The Serendipity Cabal, their entire seventeen-supercluster civilization, their eight hundred million years of evolution, their perfect probability mastery, was completely, utterly, irrevocably erased from existence, their sentience scattered into a screaming, incoherent static that would never cohere again. But the trillions of innocent civilizations caught in the crossfire.*

*He sighed. It was a soft, barely audible exhalation. And then, without moving a single muscle, without exerting the faintest glimmer of His true power, He effortlessly, instantly restored the entire omniversal quadrant to perfect, pristine condition in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillions of beings was perfectly resurrected, their contradictory states resolved, their memories of the agonizing paradox gently erased—not out of mercy, I suspect, but simply because leaving them traumatized would be untidy. The restoration was as casual as a man picking a piece of lint off his sleeve. He did not restore the Cabal. Their screaming, probability-torn fragments were left to dissolve into the quantum noise, a permanent monument to the absolute futility of trying to out-calculate a god whose luck is the fundamental axiom of existence.*

*He returned to the throne room and sat down. He looked at me with those ancient, infinite eyes, and His ruggedly handsome face broke into a warm, paternal smile. “You look pallid, Kelvyn,” He said, His rich baritone vibrating with genuine concern. “You should eat something. Have the kitchens prepare you a meal.” I bowed and thanked Him, and then I walked out of the throne room on legs that felt like hollow glass, and I vomited into the corridor, and then I came down here to write this account, and I am still writing, and I will keep writing until the trembling stops, which I do not expect to ever happen.*

---

I closed Kelvyn’s journal with hands that were no longer entirely my own. The violet luminescence beneath my skin had dimmed to a faint, sickly pulse, as if my own mana was trying to hide from the words I had just absorbed. Trillions of civilizations caught in a probability storm, their bodies torn between contradictory states, all because the King’s passive luck had brushed against a machine. And He had restored them with less effort than a blink. They would never know they had been unmade. They would never know their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical tidiness of an apex predator.

I reached for the second journal with fingers that left damp prints on the ancient leather. The year was 300,000. The handwriting was jagged, erratic, the letters slashed into the parchment with a violence that spoke of a man barely restraining himself from hurling the quill across the room.

---

**Year 300,000 — The Incident of Absolute Speed**
**Penned by High Wizard Tormas, 100th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account while my hands are still bleeding. The healers have regrown my eardrums three times in the past hour, and each time I think the ringing has stopped, I realize it is not ringing. It is screaming. The sound of time itself being violently torn apart is still echoing through the ruined architecture of my auditory cortex, and I do not believe it will ever fully fade. I will hear it in my sleep, if I ever sleep again. I will hear it when I die.*

*The opposition was called the Chrono-Stillness Covenant, and they were not warriors. They were ontological separatists, a civilization of hyper-beings who had, over the course of a billion years, achieved absolute mastery over the flow of time. They had withdrawn entirely from the linear progression of cause and effect, isolating their entire existence in a perfectly still, timeless pocket of reality where no event could ever occur, no change could ever propagate, no attack could ever reach them. From this fortress of eternal stasis, they had observed the omniverse with the detached, clinical gaze of immortals, and they had concluded, with the unshakable arrogance of beings who had never known change, that they could simply step out of their still pocket, strike Godric in the frozen instant before He could react, and then retreat back into their timeless sanctuary before any countermeasure could touch them. They were, in their own minds, perfectly, absolutely unassailable.*

*When Thronegate’s envoys arrived, the Covenant did not even emerge from their pocket. They simply extended a single, temporally isolated tendril of stasis into our reality, a finger of absolute stillness that touched the envoys’ ship and instantly froze every single molecule in a state of perfect, unchanging suspension. Our diplomats did not die. They were simply paused, their consciousness locked in the eternal, unchanging instant of their final thought, a thought that happened to be one of sudden, dawning terror. They will remain there forever, unless the King decides to unstir them. He has not yet done so.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He had been sitting motionless for twenty-two days, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber sconces, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments. I delivered the report, my voice steady but cold. He listened without expression, and then He nodded once and rose from the throne. He materialized in the void outside the Covenant’s timeless pocket, a region of absolute, unchanging stillness that no external force could penetrate because the concept of “force” depended on time to propagate.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His shields. He suppressed every single offensive capability, every stat that could be weaponized, reducing the expression of His true power by an infinite recursion of fractional negations until the remaining allowance was a value so infinitesimally small that the difference between it and absolute zero was itself an infinite, unbridgeable chasm. He allowed only the most laughably negligible fraction of a single stat to express itself. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what Speed means when it has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for three hundred thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute law of His existence that determines the maximum possible rate of change for any event in any reality, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that physics or metaphysics or ontological mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so microscopically, impossibly tiny that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque, laughable overestimation. It was a negative infinity of nothing, a phantom of a ghost of a fraction that should not have been able to affect anything at all.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift a finger. He simply allowed His heart to beat once. A single, voluntary contraction of cardiac muscle, a biological function He does not need and has not used in three hundred thousand years, a movement so effortlessly negligible that it should have produced no physical effect whatsoever. The heart muscle contracted. A single drop of His immortal blood was displaced a distance of exactly one Planck length.*

*The temporal stasis field of the Chrono-Stillness Covenant was not merely shattered. It was retroactively unmade.
