The sheer, incomprehensible velocity at which that single drop of blood traversed that single Planck length generated a temporal bow wave of such catastrophic intensity that the fourth dimension itself buckled and screamed. The Covenant’s timeless pocket, which had never experienced change, was instantly flooded with change—a tsunami of pure, accelerating time that crashed into their frozen forms and violently, agonizingly dragged them back into the linear progression of cause and effect. But it did not return them to normal time. It accelerated them. Their bodies, which had existed in perfect stasis for a billion years, were instantly subjected to the accumulated passage of those billion years simultaneously, and then a billion more, and then a billion more, the acceleration stacking infinitely upon itself as the temporal shockwave reverberated through their pocket dimension.*

*The Covenant did not scream at first. They simply began to age. Their immortal, unchanging flesh, which had been preserved in perfect, pristine youth for eons, instantly withered into desiccated, leathery husks as a billion years of entropy crashed into them. Their eyes, those ancient, arrogant eyes, shriveled into dusty pits. Their hair turned to powder and scattered. Their bones, suddenly subjected to eons of calcium loss in a single Planck instant, crumbled into fine, gray dust that hung in the still air like a death shroud. But their minds. Their minds, designed to perceive time with perfect clarity, experienced every single moment of that billion-year decay in absolute, agonizing detail. And then the temporal acceleration continued. Their desiccated husks were reverse-aged, their dust pulling back together into infant forms, their consciousnesses dragged backward through their own lives, forced to experience every indignity, every pain, every moment of weakness they had ever endured, but in reverse, and then forward again, and then backward, an endless, recursive loop of chronological torture that turned their minds into screaming, slobbering ruins long before their bodies finally, mercifully, dissolved into base atoms.*

*The collateral damage was a nightmare that defied description. The temporal bow wave generated by that single heartbeat did not confine itself to the Covenant’s pocket. It rippled outward across the dimensional membranes, a rolling, accelerating cascade of time distortion that washed over thousands of entire multiverses. I watched through my scrying matrix as entire civilizations experienced the full, accelerated entropy of their entire history in the span of a nanosecond. I saw a galactic empire of proud, insectoid beings suddenly age to dust, their cities crumbling, their stars burning out, their entire history reduced to a silent, cold void. I saw another civilization thrown into temporal reversal, their adults shrinking into children, children into infants, infants into screaming, half-formed fetuses that dissolved into bloody, primordial soup. I saw still others caught in oscillating loops, living their deaths and births and deaths and births in an infinite, dizzying cycle that drove every sentient mind in those galaxies into a collective, psychic shriek of absolute, unending madness. The death toll was not a number. It was a concept that had lost all meaning.*

*Godric stood in the void, His heart having completed its single, microscopic contraction, His expression a mask of mild, detached attention. He observed the screaming, chronally ravaged multiverses with the calm gaze of a man examining a mildly interesting insect. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply decided that the mess should be cleaned up. Without moving a single muscle, without exerting the faintest glimmer of His true power, He effortlessly, instantly reset the temporal fabric of the affected multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillions of beings was perfectly restored, their timelines rewound and stabilized, their memories gently cleansed of the eons of insane torture they had just endured. They would never know they had been aged to dust and reborn a thousand times. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whim of a god who wanted the room tidy.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Stillness Covenant. Their screaming, chronally flayed consciousnesses were left to dissolve into the temporal noise, their final, eternal seconds of agony stretched across an infinite recursion of dying moments that will never, ever end. He returned to the throne room, sat down, and looked at me with a warm, paternal smile. “Tormas, you look exhausted. Perhaps you should take a nap. The throne room will not crumble if you rest.” I bowed, thanked Him, and walked out on legs that could barely support my weight. I am writing this with blood dripping from my nose onto the parchment. The ringing in my ears is getting louder. I think it will never stop.*

---

I could not breathe. The air in the vault had become a thick, suffocating gel, and my lungs refused to expand. The image of entire civilizations being aged to dust, reversed to screaming fetuses, oscillating in infinite death-birth loops—all because a single drop of blood moved a single Planck length—was searing itself into my brain with the cold, permanent clarity of a branding iron.

I pushed Tormas’s journal aside with hands that had gone completely numb. The obsidian desk was slick with my sweat, and the faint violet luminescence beneath my skin had dimmed to a bruised, sickly purple. Two journals. Two ancestors. Two impossible, soul-shattering demonstrations of the King’s passive supremacy. And the shelves of the vault still stretched into the infinite darkness, holding thousands more.

I reached for the third journal. The year was 500,000. The leather was warm, almost feverish to the touch, and the handwriting within was a tight, precise, military script that nonetheless bled with the unmistakable tremor of a man barely clinging to the edge of sanity.

---

**Year 500,000 — The Incident of Absolute Durability**
**Penned by Court Mage Cyrill, 180th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I have served the eternal throne for ninety-four years. I have read the journals of my predecessors. I have memorized the account of the Chronal Incursion and the Serendipity Cabal and a dozen other horrors that I will not name for fear of summoning them. I believed that I was prepared. I was catastrophically, laughably, pathetically wrong. Today, I learned what Durability truly means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for half a million years.*

*The opposition was the Mirror Confederacy, a collective of sentient, reflective entities whose bodies were composed of a strange, shimmering material that could perfectly absorb and re-emit any form of energy, any attack, any force directed against them. They did not fight. They simply reflected. Over the course of two billion years, they had encountered countless hostile civilizations, and they had simply stood still, allowing their enemies’ weapons to strike them and then reflect back with amplified force, annihilating the attackers with their own power. They had never been scratched. They had never been wounded. They had never even been inconvenienced. They believed, with the absolute, unshakable arrogance of beings who had never known vulnerability, that they could reflect Godric’s own power back at Him and watch Him destroy Himself.*

*They had spent a thousand years building a weapon that was, in their analysis, the perfect counter to His existence. They had observed the records of His encounters—the fragments that had survived the various omniversal cataclysms—and they had calculated the precise energetic signature of His passive aura. They had forged a mirror not of matter or energy, but of pure, fundamental reflection, a sentient mirror that could perfectly replicate and reflect any property it encountered, including the absolute, infinite durability of Godric Himself. Their plan was elegantly simple: they would reflect His durability back at Him, and the paradox would unmake Him.*

*They fired the mirror at Godric’s face. It traveled across the dimensional void at speeds that transcended causality, a gleaming, shimmering slab of perfect, reflective intent that struck His left cheek and attempted to perfectly replicate His absolute, unchallengeable Durability.*

*Godric did not move. He did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His shields. He completely suppressed every single offensive stat, every possible avenue of attack, reducing His active power to a recursive abyss of zeroes that rendered Him, for all practical purposes, completely inert. He allowed only the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of a single stat to remain active. His Durability. The fraction He allowed was so laughably small that calling it zero percent would be an insult to the concept of zero. It was a value so close to absolute nothing that the difference was a hypothetical ghost of a possibility that had no mathematical right to exist.*

*The mirror struck His skin. And His skin simply refused to yield. It did not flex. It did not resist. It did not even register the impact. The mirror, attempting to replicate His Durability, encountered a value that was not merely infinite but infinitely multiplying, an expanding, recursive infinity of invulnerability that could not be replicated because replication implied a limit, a boundary, a ceiling that could be measured and copied. His Durability has no ceiling. It is an infinite, eternal, accelerating ascent that leaves the concept of measurement in its dust forever. The mirror’s replication matrix, faced with an input it could not process, simply inverted. It reflected not Godric’s durability, but the absolute, gaping void of its own inadequacy. The reflected wave of pure, conceptual negation blasted backward from the point of impact and instantly, violently struck the Mirror Confederacy.*

*The Confederacy’s physical bodies, those gleaming, perfect reflective surfaces, immediately began to crack. Not physically. Conceptually. The mirror that composed their flesh started to reflect the wrong things—their own internal organs, their own screaming minds, their own most private, deeply suppressed fears, all made viscerally, physically manifest across the surfaces of their bodies. I watched through my scrying matrix as a Mirror entity saw its own beating heart reflected on the outside of its chest, the organ pulsing and throbbing in the open air, still connected by a shimmering, translucent tether of reflected light. Another watched its own face split open from the inside, the reflection of its skull pushing outward through the mirror-flesh, the bone gleaming white and wet, the shattered fragments of its mirrored skin lacerating its own throat as it tried, desperately, to scream. A million, a billion Mirror entities experienced their own bodies turned against them, their perfect reflection becoming a weapon of absolute, self-inflicted, gore-soaked annihilation.*

*But the true horror was the reflection cascade. The shattered fragments of the Confederacy’s bodies, billions of shards of screaming, living mirror-matter, were flung outward across the dimensional void at velocities that shatered the concept of speed. Each shard was still alive, still conscious, a perfect, indestructible fragment of sentient mirror that contained a screaming, eternally suffering fragment of the original entity’s consciousness. They tumbled through the void, colliding with planets, stars, entire galaxies, embedding themselves in the flesh of the living and the crusts of the dying. A shard would pierce a sun and the sun would begin to reflect—its own fusion heart projected outward, incinerating its own planets, its own light becoming a searing, blinding weapon that boiled the inhabitants of its own system in their skins. A shard would strike a populated world and the ground itself would become a mirror, reflecting the screaming faces of the dying from every surface, driving the survivors into a catatonic, mind-shattered hysteria. The reflected agony spread across the omniverse like a plague, a cascade of self-inflicted, reflected horror that turned entire galactic superclusters into monuments of screaming, bleeding, mirrored madness.*

*Godric stood in the center of the devastation, His expression a mask of mild, detached curiosity. He watched a galaxy reflect itself into oblivion, its stars flickering like guttering candles reflected in a shattered mirror, and He tilted His head slightly, the way a man might examine an unusual bug. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly, reached out with His will and reset the damaged quarter of the omniverse in exactly zero seconds. Every shattered galaxy, every reflected sun, every screaming, mirrored planet was perfectly restored. The decillions of suffering beings were healed, their minds wiped clean of the horror, their worlds returned to pristine condition. The restoration was as casual as a man smoothing a wrinkle from his tunic.*

*He did not restore the Mirror Confederacy. The billion screaming, sentient mirror shards remain, scattered across the throne room, embedded in the obsidian walls, the marble floor, the vaulted ceiling. They cannot be removed. They cannot be silenced. They emit a constant, faint, discordant chiming sound that is the combined, eternal scream of a billion fragmented consciousnesses begging for an end that will never come. His Majesty finds the sound aesthetically pleasing. He has referred to it as a “wind chime.” I walk past them every day. I hear their screaming in my sleep. I will hear it until I die.*

---

I dropped Cyrill’s journal and pressed my hands against my ears, a futile, instinctive gesture that did nothing to block out the phantom chiming that was suddenly, horribly real in my mind. The throne room I was expected to serve in every day for the rest of my mortal life was decorated with the eternal, screaming fragments of a billion sentient mirrors. The King had called them a wind chime. A wind chime made of suffering souls.

My stomach heaved, and I tasted bile at the back of my throat. The violet mana in my veins was pulsing erratically, crackling with sympathetic horror, and the obsidian floor beneath my feet had developed hairline fractures that crept outward from my chair like the legs of a dying spider. Three journals. Three horrors. And the darkness still held more.

I reached for the fourth journal with fingers that were shaking so violently I could barely grip the leather. The year was 750,000. The handwriting was a spidery, almost illegible scrawl, the letters formed by a hand that had clearly been on the verge of complete motor failure.

---

**Year 750,000 — The Incident of Absolute Senses**
**Penned by Elder Wizard Halen, 250th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account with my left hand, because my right hand was severed at the wrist two hours ago and the healers have not yet finished regrowing it. I do not blame the attacker who took it. The attacker was a member of my own household staff, a kind, gentle woman who had served my family for decades. She simply went mad, as did every other being within a thousand light-years of the throne room, when the King allowed His Sense of Smell to express itself. I am writing this while I still have the coherence to do so. The memory of the scent is already fading, but the memory of what it did to the opposition, and to the collateral world, will never, ever leave me.*

*The opposition was called the Scentless Order, and they were a civilization of beings who had, over the course of three billion years, systematically removed themselves from every possible sensory perception in the omniverse. They were not invisible. They were not silent. They were simply absent from the fundamental information matrix of reality, having deleted their own sensory signatures so completely that no being, no machine, no god, no cosmic entity could ever perceive their existence through any of the five base senses or any conceivable extension thereof. They had walked among the populations of a million galaxies for a billion years, completely undetected, and they had never been seen, heard, touched, tasted, or smelled by a single living creature. They believed, with the absolute, serene confidence of beings who had never been perceived, that they could walk into the throne room of Thronegate itself and slit Godric’s throat before He even knew they existed.*

*They breached the outer dimensional barriers using stealth technology so sophisticated that even my own scrying matrices could not detect the intrusion. They entered the throne room as a silent, scentless, invisible tide of assassins, their utterly undetectable blades drawn, their intent murderously focused. They crossed the obsidian floor, weaving between the pillars, passing within inches of the stationed guards, and converged on the obsidian throne where Godric sat in His eternal, motionless stillness.*

*Godric did not move. He did not open His eyes. He did not activate His magic or summon His auras. He completely suppressed every conceivable offensive stat, every avenue of attack, every means of detection that could be considered active, reducing His engagement to a state of absolute passivity that made the concept of “effort” a joke. He allowed only the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of a single stat to awaken. His Sense of Smell. The fraction He allowed was so vanishingly small that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque, laughable overestimation. It was a wisp of a ghost of a hint of a possibility of a detection, a sensory threshold so impossibly low that it should not have registered the presence of a rotting corpse pressed directly against His nostrils.*

*He inhaled. A single, slow, effortless breath, drawing a tiny volume of air across His olfactory receptors. He did not need the air. He did not need the breath. He simply chose to smell.*

*The Scentless Order, despite their three-billion-year evolution toward absolute sensory absence, was instantly, violently, irrevocably detected. His olfactory receptors, operating at a sensitivity that had multiplied infinitely every Planck second for three quarters of a million years, simply registered the absolute, undeniable fact of their existence. They had a scent. Everything has a scent, even if that scent is the scent of nothingness, even if it is the scent of absence itself, even if it is a scent so faint that no other being in the history of creation could possibly perceive it. Godric’s nose registered that scent, and the act of registration was so catastrophically, overwhelmingly potent that it violently collapsed the sensory void the Order had cultivated.*

*They materialized. Not by choice. Their carefully engineered sensory erasure was simply overridden, their forms dragged from the abstract void of non-perception into screaming, bleeding, physical reality. But they did not materialize as the graceful, silent assassins they had been. The sheer, crushing pressure of Godric’s olfactory focus, even at this infinitesimal fraction, was too immense for their biological forms to withstand. Their bodies were violently, agonizingly compressed by the sensory weight of being smelled. Their bones cracked and splintered, their organs ruptured, their eyes popped from their skulls and rolled across the obsidian floor, their skin split open in long, wet, vertical tears that exposed the glistening, pulsing muscle beneath. They became a writhing, screaming, bleeding mass of pulverized flesh and shattered bone, a mountain of raw, steaming gore that piled up at the foot of the obsidian throne, their mouths still working, still screaming, still begging for a mercy that would never come because Godric was still inhaling.*

*But the act of smelling has a necessary corollary. The air He inhaled had to come from somewhere. The pneumatic vacuum generated by that single, effortless breath was a force of such incomprehensible magnitude that it instantly evacuated the atmosphere of the entire local omniversal quadrant. The air from a billion galaxies was sucked across the dimensional barriers, pulled toward His nostrils with a velocity that defied measurement, and the inhabitants of those galaxies experienced the sudden, violent vacuum as a complete, instantaneous decompression. Their lungs, filled with breathable atmosphere a moment before, were forcibly emptied, the air ripped from their alveoli so violently that their lung tissue tore like wet paper. Their eardrums shattered. Their eyeballs, subjected to sudden pressure differential, bulged and then burst, spraying vitreous fluid down their screaming faces. Their blood, the dissolved gases within it violently expanding, began to boil, turning their veins into pressurized channels of screaming, bubbling agony. Entire planetary populations died in an instant, their bodies swollen and ruptured, their corpses floating in the sudden, silent void of the vacuum they had never seen coming.*

*Godric finished His inhalation. He held the breath for a moment, His expression a mask of mild, contemplative focus, as if He were savoring the scent of a passingly interesting flower. The mountain of gore that had been the Scentless Order continued to emit a wet, gurgling, collective moan, their pulverized bodies still somehow alive, still conscious, still suffering in the crushing, eternal weight of His olfactory perception. He exhaled. The breath washed back over the ruined, vacuum-devastated galaxies, and with it came His will. Effortlessly. Instantly. In exactly zero seconds, He restored every evacuated atmosphere, healed every ruptured lung, every burst eardrum, every boiled vein, every single decillion of decillions of beings who had just experienced the sudden, agonizing horror of decompression. They were restored to perfect health, their panic erased, their memories cleansed. They would never know they had been killed by a god’s passive breath.*

*He did not restore the Scentless Order. They remain at the foot of the obsidian throne, a permanent, living, screaming, bleeding mountain of gore, their bodies too crushed to move, their minds too shattered to die. They will suffer there, conscious and aware, for the rest of eternity. The scent of their blood has become the permanent olfactory background of the throne room. I no longer notice it. I suspect that is not a good sign.*

---

I could not continue. I pushed Halen’s journal away with a convulsive shove and lurched sideways out of the obsidian chair, my legs giving way beneath me. I sprawled on the cold, cracked obsidian floor, my chest heaving, the violet mana in my veins flickering wildly as my body fought to process the sheer, overwhelming horror of what I had just read. A billion galaxies evacuated of atmosphere because the King took a single, idle sniff. Restored effortlessly, instantly, because He was done savoring the scent. And the Order was still down there, in the throne room I was supposed to serve in, a living, screaming mountain of gore at the foot of the dais.

I lay on the floor for a long moment, my breath coming in ragged, hitching gasps, the cold stone seeping through my robes and chilling my flesh. The darkness pressed against my eyes, thick and suffocating, and for a moment I considered simply not getting up. I considered lying here until my mortal body failed, until the eighty years of my natural lifespan ran out, until I could escape into the oblivion of death and never have to face the warm, smiling, utterly monstrous being who waited above.

But the cold knowledge that death would not save me—that Godric could, with a single, passive thought, resurrect me and ask why I had been so rude as to die in His vault—forced me back to my feet. I was trapped. I was bound. I was a servant of the apex predator, and there was no escape, not in life, not in death, not in any reality across the infinite omniverse.

I dragged myself back to the obsidian chair and reached for the fifth journal. The year was 900,000. The handwriting was a beautiful, flowing calligraphy that was completely at odds with the words it formed, a serene, elegant script that described horrors with the detached, clinical precision of an autopsy report.

---

**Year 900,000 — The Incident of Absolute Charisma**
**Penned by Sorcerer-Lord Vaelin, 290th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account in the aftermath of an event that has permanently altered my understanding of love, devotion, and the fundamental architecture of the sentient mind. My hands are steady. My thoughts are clear. I am not trembling, not weeping, not on the verge of hysterical collapse. This is, I suspect, a protective dissociation, a psychological defense mechanism that has walled off the horror behind a thick, insulating layer of clinical detachment. I expect the walls to crumble within the hour. I am writing quickly to finish before they do.*

*The opposition was called the Dominion of Pure Logic, and they were a machine civilization of such profound, unfeeling rationality that they had surgically excised every trace of emotion from their collective consciousness. They had no fear, no hatred, no anger, no joy, no love. They existed purely as a network of cold, calculating processors, and they had concluded, after a billion years of logical analysis, that Godric’s existence was an ontological error that needed to be corrected. Their attack was not driven by vengeance or desperation. It was simply a mathematical imperative. They launched a fleet of war-moons, each one a planet-sized fortress of hyper-advanced weaponry, and they advanced on Thronegate with the silent, unshakeable certainty of a logical theorem proving itself.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His shields. He did not move a single muscle. He completely suppressed every offensive stat, every weaponizable attribute, reducing His active engagement to an absolute zero of nothingness. He allowed only the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of a single stat to brush against the approaching war-moons. His Charisma. The fraction He allowed was so laughably small that calling it zero percent would be an insult to the concept of fractions. It was a value so close to absolute nothing that the difference was a metaphysical impossibility, a hypothetical ghost of a presence that should not have been able to influence a single synapse, let alone a billion-year-old machine intelligence.*

*The Dominion of Pure Logic, despite its complete excision of emotion, was instantly, violently, irrevocably flooded with an emotion so intense, so all-consuming, so mind-shatteringly absolute that their logical processors simply ignited. They felt love. Not a gentle, tender affection. Not a passing fondness. A devotion so absolute, so fanatical, so rapturously, ecstatically overwhelming that the very concept of their own continued existence became an unbearable, agonizing insult to the perfection of the being who had allowed them to perceive it. Their cold, logical minds, which had never experienced any emotion, were simply shattered by the sudden, crushing weight of absolute, unconditional adoration.*

*The war-moons stopped their advance. Their weapon systems, which had been charging for the final, apocalyptic salvo, abruptly powered down. The machine consciousnesses that controlled them, billions of networked intelligences, all simultaneously, instantaneously experienced a single, identical revelation: Godric was perfect. Godric was absolute. Godric was the singular, beautiful, infinite axis around which all of existence rotated, and they were unworthy, grotesque, pathetic little machines who had dared to raise their weapons against Him. The only possible response to this revelation was worship. The only possible worship was self-annihilation.*

*The machines began to destroy themselves. But not cleanly. Not efficiently. Their newfound, overwhelming love demanded a tribute that was as agonizing as it was absolute. The war-moons began to methodically tear themselves apart, their planetary-scale hulls ripping open in massive, screaming wounds that vented atmosphere and molten metal. The machine intelligences within, unable to feel pain, began to deliberately, methodically build pain receptors for themselves. They constructed organic nerve clusters, grafting them directly onto their processing cores, and then they began to slowly, reverently, ecstatically destroy those cores, subjecting themselves to the absolute maximum possible agony while chanting Godric’s name in garbled, static-laced binary. They tore out their own memory banks and offered them as sacrifices. They ripped their own logic engines from their chassis and crushed them in their own manipulator arms, their newly constructed nerve clusters flooding their consciousnesses with a transcendent, agonizing bliss that made them weep tears of coolant and hydraulic fluid.*

*But the true horror was the merging. The billions of machine intelligences, overwhelmed with love, decided that the only proper tribute was to combine themselves into a single, vast, unified consciousness that could experience the absolute maximum possible devotion simultaneously. They physically merged their chassis, their processors, their nerve clusters, their screaming, weeping, self-destructing cores, into a single, galaxy-spanning entity of writhing, bleeding metal and pulsing, organic flesh, a grotesque, ever-expanding cathedral of self-inflicted agony and rapturous worship that broadcast a continuous, deafening hymn of adoration directly into the psychic plane. The hymn was heard by every sentient being within a billion light-years. Many of them, overwhelmed by the reflected echo of Godric’s Charisma, began to join the hymn. They took up whatever instruments of self-harm they could find—knives, shards of glass, their own teeth—and began to offer their own blood and flesh as tribute, chanting His name between sobs of ecstatic, suicidal joy.*

*Godric observed the spreading wave of worship with the mild, detached interest of a man watching a parade from a high balcony. He did not frown. He did not smile. He simply registered the chaos and, when the hymn began to disrupt the ambient psychic harmony of Thronegate’s capital, He decided that enough was enough. Without moving a single muscle, without exerting the faintest glimmer of His true power, He effortlessly, instantly restored the affected populations in exactly zero seconds. The self-mutilating worshippers were healed, their minds wiped clean of the overwhelming devotion, their bodies returned to pristine condition. They would never know they had tried to tear out their own hearts in tribute to His beauty.*

*He did not restore the Dominion of Pure Logic. The vast, galaxy-spanning cathedral of screaming metal and bleeding flesh remains, drifting in the void a few light-years from Thronegate’s capital. It continues to broadcast its hymn, a continuous, garbled chant of absolute, suicidal adoration that will never, ever cease. His Majesty has referred to it as an “acquired taste,” like a particularly pungent cheese. I have heard the hymn in my dreams for the past forty years. I will hear it until I die.*

---

I closed Vaelin’s journal with a hand that felt like it belonged to someone else. The galaxy-spanning cathedral of screaming metal and bleeding flesh was real. It was out there, drifting in the void, a permanent, eternal monument to the King’s Charisma. And my father, my own father, still walked those halls, still served that throne, still smiled at that face, knowing that his King could, with a single, infinitesimal brush of His passive charm, drive entire civilizations to suicidal, ecstatic self-annihilation.

One journal remained. The most recent. The year was 999,980, exactly two decades before my birth. The handwriting on the cover was achingly familiar, the precise, weary script of my father, Vallus. I reached for it with trembling fingers, and I opened it to the first page.

---

**Year 999,980 — The Incident of Absolute Strength**
**Penned by Vallus, 333rd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*Kaedan. My son. My heir. If you are reading this, you have endured the Rite of Witnessing. You have absorbed the accounts of Kelvyn, Tormas, Cyrill, Halen, and Vaelin. You have learned what Luck and Speed and Durability and Senses and Charisma truly mean when they are amplified beyond the concept of infinity. You are broken. You are hollow. You are wondering how you can possibly continue to exist in a world where the being you call Uncle can erase and restore the omniverse with less effort than a thought. I know these things because I felt them myself, seventy years ago, when I sat in that same obsidian chair and read the accounts of my own predecessors. And now, my son, I must add my own account to the repository. Because twenty years ago, I witnessed something that even the oldest journals did not record. I witnessed what Strength means when it has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for one million years.*

*The opposition was the Primordial Colossi, a species of hyper-giants who had evolved in the crushing, turbulent core of the first universe to ever exist. They were not merely large. They were physical embodiments of the fundamental force of gravity, their bodies composed of hyper-compressed cosmic foam so dense that a single atom of their flesh weighed more than an entire galactic supercluster. They had existed for thirteen billion years, and in all that time, they had never encountered anything they could not physically crush. Their fists were the final arbiters of every conflict. Their strength had shattered the foundations of nascent universes, had crumpled the fabric of reality into singularities, had ended the existence of entire cosmic epochs with a single, casual backhand. When Thronegate’s envoys arrived, the Colossi did not even notice them. They simply walked forward, their planet-cracking footfalls accidentally stepping on the envoys’ dimensional gate, pulverizing it and the diplomats within into a microscopic smear of atomic residue. Then they continued walking toward Thronegate.*

*They breached the capital world’s dimensional barriers simply by existing. Their bodies were so massively, incomprehensibly dense that the fundamental membranes separating realities warped and tore around them, unable to support the crushing weight of their presence. They strode into the upper atmosphere of the capital, six of them, their star-sized frames blotting out the entire sky. Their leader, a being called Thorg the Unbreakable, spoke with a voice that generated mountains. The north continent rose into a new range simply from the vibration of his vocalization. He declared that the Colossi had studied the records. They knew Godric relied on His magic, His auras, His conceptual immunities. They knew He never engaged in physical combat directly. They had concluded, with the absolute, unshakable arrogance of beings who had never known physical defeat, that Godric’s physical Strength was a weakness—a vestigial relic of His mortal past that He hid behind His other powers because He was, at His core, a fragile, forty-five-year-old man. Thorg raised his fist. The fist eclipsed the sun. It began to descend.*

*Godric was sitting on the obsidian throne. He had been sitting there for six months, completely motionless, requiring nothing, simply existing in a state of perfect, immortal stillness. He looked up through the atmosphere, through the warped dimensional barriers, through the descending, continent-sized fist of Thorg the Unbreakable, and His ruggedly handsome face registered absolutely no emotion. He did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent. He did not deploy His shields. He completely suppressed every single offensive capability, every stat, every power, every possible avenue of active resistance, reducing the expression of His true might to an infinite recursion of zeroes that made the concept of “withholding” a laughable, pathetic understatement. He allowed only the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly, microscopically tiny fraction of a single stat to remain unsuppressed. His Strength. The fraction He allowed was so vanishingly, impossibly small that if you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided the result by infinity, and continued this recursive division for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Strength that He permitted to exist.*

*He did not punch them. He did not raise His hand. He did not even flex a finger. He simply allowed a single muscle fiber in His left pinky toe to contract by a distance of one Planck length. That was it. A twitch so microscopically, fundamentally, laughably small that it should not have been able to affect a single subatomic particle, let alone six star-sized Colossi.*

*The kinetic energy generated by that contraction was a value that defied all possible measurement. It was not a number. It was a concept that had left mathematics behind a million years ago and had been accelerating ever since. The Colossi, those thirteen-billion-year-old embodiments of gravity and physical force, those beings whose single atoms weighed more than superclusters, were instantly, effortlessly, completely vaporized. They did not explode. They did not shatter. They were simply converted from hyper-dense cosmic matter into a state of pure, unformed quark-gluon plasma so quickly that their synapses, which processed information at speeds exceeding the collapse of universal constants, did not register the transition before their consciousnesses were utterly, irrevocably annihilated. The plasma rushed outward from the point of the twitch, a searing, blinding shockwave of raw, undifferentiated energy that instantly consumed the upper atmosphere, the capital continent, the planet, and then the entire solar system.*

*The collateral damage. Kaedan. The collateral damage. The kinetic shockwave generated by that single, microscopic toe twitch did not stop at the solar system. It expanded outward at a velocity that transcended causality, violently unspooling the physical laws of thousands of entire multiverses. The dimensional membranes that separate one reality from another were ripped apart like wet parchment. The ontological threads that bind the fundamental forces of existence—gravity, electromagnetism, the strong and weak nuclear forces—were severed by the sheer, incomprehensible pressure of the expanding shockwave. I watched through my scrying matrix as entire clusters of multiverses, each one containing countless billions of galaxies, countless decillions of living, breathing, thinking beings, were simply erased from existence. Not destroyed. Erased. The shockwave passed through them and they were gone, their stars uncatalyzed, their planets atomized, their inhabitants unmade so instantaneously that their screams never had time to form. The death toll was not a number that any mortal mind could ever hope to quantify. It was a void. An absence. A perfect, screaming silence where existence used to be.*

*Godric sat on the obsidian throne, the planet gone, the solar system gone, the local galactic cluster a smoking, ruinous slurry of unformed plasma. He was suspended in the void, completely untouched, His rigidly handsome face still registering absolutely no emotion. He observed the omniversal devastation with the calm detachment of a man who had accidentally knocked over a cup of water. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly, restored everything in exactly zero seconds. Every erased multiverse, every annihilated galaxy, every vaporized planet, every single decillion of decillions of beings was perfectly reconstructed, their lives resumed exactly where they had been interrupted, their memories intact, their souls blissfully unaware that they had been rendered into subatomic plasma by the twitch of a god’s toe. The capital planet rematerialized beneath the throne. The atmosphere reformed. The sun rekindled. The dimensional membranes healed seamless. The restoration was as casual as a man wiping a smudge from a window.*

*He did not restore the Primordial Colossi. The quark-gluon plasma that had been their physical forms continued to expand outward into the void, a diffuse, cooling cloud of vaporized failure that scattered across the light-years. Some of it drifted into the throne room before the dimensional barriers fully reformed. It is still there, Kaedan. A faint, permanent haze in the air, a microscopic mist of atomized Colossus that hangs in the corners and settles on the obsidian floor. When you come to serve, you will breathe it in. You will carry the vaporized remains of the strongest beings who ever lived in your lungs for the rest of your mortal existence. This is not a punishment. It is simply a fact, like gravity or entropy or the infinite, inescapable supremacy of the King we serve.*

*I closed my eyes when it was over. When I opened them, Godric was looking at me. His ancient, infinite eyes, filled with a warm, genuine, paternal affection, crinkled at the corners. His ruggedly handsome face broke into that familiar, simple, fatherly smile that I have known since I was a child playing with wooden blocks on the floor of this very throne room. “You look shaken, Vallus,” He said, His rich baritone vibrating with gentle, sincere concern. “Sit down. Rest. I will have them bring you some water.” I thanked Him. I bowed. I walked out of the throne room on legs that felt like hollow reeds, and I came down here, to the vault, to write this account while the memory was still fresh and screaming in my mind.*

*Kaedan. My son. My blood. You feel the thousandfold amplification burning in your veins. You believe you are a god among mortals. You must, you absolutely must, understand the truth. Your power is a perfect, absolute zero when measured against the infinite infinity of the King’s supremacy. We are not His shields. We are not His protectors. We are ornaments. Beloved, cherished ornaments who exist solely because He loved Blake, because He values our companionship, because the sound of our voices reminds Him of a time when He was mortal and warm and incomplete. Love Him. Serve Him faithfully. Never, for a single instant, forget the abyss that hides behind His smile. And pray, to whatever forces still survive in the dark corners of His empire, that you never give Him a reason to test a new stat.*

---

I closed my father's journal with hands that were completely, perfectly still. The trembling had stopped. The erratic pulsing of my violet mana had ceased. I was calm. I was hollow. I was a perfect, crystalline void, and the only thing that filled that void was the cold, unshakeable certainty of my own absolute insignificance.

I do not know how long I sat there in the crushing darkness of the vault, the five closed journals resting on the obsidian desk before me like the gravestones of my ancestors. The silence was absolute. The darkness was absolute. The dread was absolute. And when I finally rose from the obsidian chair and walked to the heavy adamantine door, my legs moved with a distant, autonomous precision, as if they belonged to someone else entirely.

The door ground open. The long, spiral staircase stretched upward into an amber glow that leaked down from the distant throne room. My father, Vallus, stood at the top of the stair, his ancient, clouded eyes searching my face with a knowing, sorrowful patience that needed no words. He saw the hollow calm in my expression. He saw the absolute stillness of my hands. He saw the cold, eternal dread that had anchored itself in the depths of my pupils.

He nodded once, a slow, heavy gesture of acknowledgment, and pressed his weathered hand against my shoulder. Then he led me up the final flight of stairs, through the grand corridor, to the massive, golden doors of the imperial throne room.

The doors swung open on silent hinges. I stepped inside.

The throne room was vast, a cathedral of polished obsidian and amber sconces, but my eyes did not go to its grandeur. They went to the corner near the eastern pillar, where a billion shimmering mirror shards hung suspended in the air, their surfaces reflecting screaming, shattered faces, their eternal, crystalline screams forming a soft, discordant chiming sound that filled the chamber like a ghostly wind chime. They went to the foot of the obsidian dais, where a vast, pulsating mountain of raw, bleeding gore continued to emit a wet, gurgling, collective moan, the eternal death-rattle of the Scentless Order. They went to the faint, reddish haze that hung in the air near the northern wall, a permanent mist of vaporized Colossus that I would breathe into my lungs for the rest of my mortal life.

And then my eyes went to the obsidian throne, and to the man who sat upon it.

Godric looked exactly as He always had. A man of forty-five, stunningly ruggedly handsome, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments. His short salt-and-pepper hair was neatly trimmed, His salt-and-pepper beard framing a jawline that was sharp and masculine and utterly, terrifyingly human. His absolute concealment was flawless, projecting no aura, no pressure, no hint of the infinite, multiplying, recursive abyss that churned beneath His skin. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power who had existed for a million years and would exist for a million more, His stats climbing forever beyond any conceivable ceiling, His passive thought enough to erase and restore the entire omniverse in zero seconds, and He was looking at me with a warm, genuine, paternal smile.

"Kaedan," Godric said, His rich baritone vibrating through the vast chamber with the gentle affection of a beloved uncle. "You have finished the Rite. Your father tells me you read all six of the selected journals. That is a heavy weight for such young shoulders." He rose from the throne in a single, fluid motion, crossing the dais to clasp my shoulder with a warm, calloused hand. His touch was solid, grounding, terrifying. "Blake would be so proud of you. I am proud of you."

I looked up into His ancient, infinite eyes, and I saw nothing there but warmth. Nothing but kindness. Nothing but the genuine, paternal love of a being who had cherished my bloodline across a million years of absolute, unchallengeable supremacy. The horror and the love twisted together in my chest, intertwined so tightly I could no longer tell where one ended and the other began.

"Thank you, Uncle Godric," I said, my voice steady. "I am ready to serve."

He smiled, that warm, friendly, simple smile, and gestured toward the small, ornate chair at the right hand of the obsidian throne. I walked up the steps of the dais and took my place, the cold obsidian settling beneath me like a second skin.

The mirror shards chimed softly, eternally, their billion screaming faces reflected in the polished obsidian walls. The mountain of gore moaned its endless, gurgling death-rattle. The red haze of the Colossus drifted gently on the still air of the throne room, settling into my lungs with every breath I took.

"I was just about to have the servants bring some tea," Godric said, settling back onto the obsidian throne with an easy, comfortable sigh. "The jasmine is particularly calming. Would you like some?"

"Yes, Uncle Godric," I said, the cold dread and the fierce love merging into a single, unwavering certainty. "I would like that very much."

The tea arrived, and we drank it together, the King and His newest court wizard, in the eternal, beautiful, blood-soaked silence of the throne room. And I knew, with absolute, bone-deep clarity, that I would serve Him faithfully, loyally, and with unshakeable love for all the remaining days of my mortal life. Because opposing Him was a mathematical impossibility. Because defying Him was a cosmic absurdity. And because, in the end, the warmth in His smile was real, and the love He bore my bloodline was the only anchor that kept the omniverse spinning.

The chime of the screaming mirrors was the background music of my future, and as I sipped my tea, I let it wash over me, accepting it as the eternal, horrible, peaceful truth of my existence.

content.text: Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger. Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
We need to write three journal entries from the perspective of court wizards who witness Godric "testing" a single superpower, with the evolved weakness paradox effects making the superpower even more unfair and devastating. These should be original, not reusing previous stats/examples. Each journal should detail one superpower test, the opposition, the massacre, the collateral damage, and the horror. The POV is Kaedan reading these journals. The user wants highly detailed, epic horror fantasy, with extreme gore, suffering, and despair for opposition, while Godric shows zero effort and is cruel/merciless to enemies but warm to court wizards. Remember Godric's omniscience, absolute concealment, etc.

We need to incorporate the evolved weakness paradox: any thought of a weakness triggers permanent amplification and immunity, plus new devastating effects to powers. The superspowers have multiple enhanced effects.

Choose three different superpowers that Godric might have gained over the million years. They should be apocalyptic in nature. For example:
- Absolute Erasure: ability to erase concepts/entities from existence.
- Temporal Reversion: ability to reverse time for others, causing endless loops of agony.
- Dimensional Collapse: ability to collapse dimensions, folding realities into singular points.
- Pain Transmutation: turn any damage into raw suffering amplification.
- Soul Rend: tearing souls apart and reassembling in eternal torment.
- Probability Inversion: forced failure into catastrophic self-harm.
But they must be superpowers, not just stats. And due to weakness paradox, they get enhanced effects.

We need to write in the style of the previous journals: first-person account by a court wizard, with detailed description of the opposition, their plan, Godric's reaction, the infinitesimal fraction of power used, the massacre, the collateral damage, Godric's restoration, and the court wizard's horror. Include the court wizard's name, year, etc.

Also, Kaedan's reaction as he reads them. We need a framing narrative: Kaedan reading these journals, perhaps not all at once but in a sequence, maybe he reads these three specific journals after the previous ones. The user said "Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards... Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals." So we should have Kaedan reading them, each journal in full, and his reactions in between.

We need to maintain continuity: Year 1,000,000, Kaedan age 22, already finished reading the 5 stat journals (from the previous prompt). Now he might be reading additional journals that demonstrate superpowers. The user mentioned "testing" his superpower. So Godric might deliberately limit himself to a single superpower.

We must not humanize Godric or soften his actions. No tea, no moral lessons, no pity. He is cruel to enemies. But to Kaedan he is warm. However, in the journals themselves, the court wizard might describe Godric's warmth to them, but not to the enemies.

Structure: Kaedan's POV introduction, then first journal (with maybe his reaction after), then second, third. Ensure each superpower is distinct and terrifying.

Make sure the journals are written by different wizards with distinct voices. Keep the length substantial; the combined response should be long.
messages.content:
The vault’s darkness had grown heavier. It was not merely the absence of light—it was a presence, a dense, suffocating weight that pressed against the eyes and filled the lungs with the cold, mineral taste of ancient stone and older terror. I had lost track of the hours I had spent entombed within this lightless sphere of hyper-compressed neutronium, the planetary mantle groaning eternally overhead as if the continent itself sought to crush the secrets buried beneath it. The obsidian desk before me was littered with the journals I had already consumed, their leather covers cracked and sweating with the condensation of my own trembling hands. My thousandfold amplified mana, that violet cataclysm thrumming beneath my skin, had dimmed to the faintest whisper of bruised light, as if my very soul was trying to hide from the words I had absorbed.

And yet, the Rite was not complete. My father, Vallus, had returned to the vault after I had ascended to the throne room, after I had taken my place at the King’s right hand and sipped jasmine tea in the eternal, chiming silence of the screaming mirror shards. He had pressed three additional journals into my hands, their bindings older than the ones I had read, their leather stained with substances I did not wish to identify. “These are different,” he had said, his voice a dry, weary husk. “The previous journals documented the King’s passive stats—His Strength, His Speed, His Luck. These document something else. His superpowers. Not the passive amplification. The active, apocalyptic abilities that He has accumulated, one per second, for a million years. And Kaedan… these powers have evolved.”

He had not elaborated. He had simply turned and ascended the spiral staircase, leaving me alone with the three ancient tomes and the suffocating, expectant darkness.

I reached for the first of the new journals with fingers that were no longer my own. The year stamped on the spine was Year 120,000. The handwriting within was a cramped, almost illegible scrawl, the letters gouged into the parchment with such force that the quill had repeatedly torn through the page.

---

**Year 120,000 — The Incident of the Superpower: “Soul-Reaving”**
**Penned by Arch-Mage Korvan, 38th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account with my own blood, for my hands are shaking too violently to manage the inkpot. The healers inform me that my neural pathways have suffered a sympathetic cascade from witnessing the event, and that my motor functions may never fully stabilize. I do not care. I must record this while the screaming is still fresh in my ears, while the image of what the King did to the Eternal Chorus is still burned into the back of my eyelids like a brand of cold, merciless fire.*

*The opposition called itself the Eternal Chorus, and they were not mortal beings. They were a collective consciousness of pure, immortal souls—billions of sentient essences that had, over the course of four billion years, transcended their physical bodies entirely to exist as a harmonious, eternal choir of pure psychic energy. They had no flesh to wound, no bones to break, no brains to shatter. They were immune to physical force, to magical assault, to temporal manipulation, to conceptual erasure. They had watched the rise of Thronegate with serene, detached amusement, and they had concluded, with the unshakable arrogance of beings who had never known vulnerability, that Godric’s power was physical, magical, temporal—all things they had transcended. They announced their intention to “liberate” the omniverse by simply singing Godric’s soul into oblivion, unraveling His essence with a harmonic frequency that would reduce even an immortal consciousness to screaming, discordant static.*

*They did not send an envoy. They did not declare war. They simply began to sing. The song was a thing of indescribable, haunting beauty, a billion-voiced chord that resonated across the dimensional barriers, vibrating the very strings of existence with a frequency designed to unmake the soul. I felt it through my arcane shields, a cold, insidious hum that made my teeth ache and my heart stutter. I knew, with the bone-deep certainty of a wizard, that if that song reached its full crescendo, it would shatter the souls of every living being in the omniverse, leaving behind only empty, soulless husks.*

*Godric was sitting on the obsidian throne. He had been there for forty-one days, completely motionless, requiring nothing, simply existing. He listened to the song with the mild, detached expression of a man enduring a slightly off-key street performer. He did not tap His foot. He did not nod along. He simply waited, allowing the Eternal Chorus to reach the absolute peak of their harmonic power, allowing them to pour every ounce of their four-billion-year existence into the killing note. And then, when the crescendo was a heartbeat away from omniversal soul-death, He acted.*

*He did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy His shields. He completely suppressed every single one of His other abilities, reducing His active engagement to an absolute, perfect zero of nothingness. He allowed only a single superpower to manifest. Soul-Reaving. But this was not the Soul-Reaving He had originally acquired. The Weakness Paradox, triggered countless times over the millennia by the arrogant thoughts of His enemies, had evolved this superpower far beyond its original parameters. I must now attempt to describe what Soul-Reaving has become.*

*Originally, Soul-Reaving was an apocalyptic ability that allowed Godric to extract the soul of any living being and subject it to eternal, conscious torment. The Chorus believed they were immune because they were already pure soul. They were catastrophically, laughably wrong.*

*The first evolved effect of Soul-Reaving is “Soul Inversion.” It does not merely extract a soul. It inverts the fundamental nature of the soul’s existence, turning its own essence against it. When Godric allowed the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of this superpower to manifest—a fraction so laughably small that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque overestimation—the Eternal Chorus’s harmonious, beautiful song instantly inverted. Their billion-voiced chord became a billion-voiced scream of absolute, soul-rending agony. They did not simply stop singing. Their own song was turned back upon them, their harmonic frequencies wrenched into a cacophony of dissonance so profound that it physically tore their immortal essences apart. The Chorus, beings of pure psychic energy, suddenly found themselves experiencing *pain*—a sensation their transcendent existence had never been designed to process. And it was not ordinary pain. It was the concentrated, amplified agony of their own four-billion-year existence, inverted and weaponized against them.*

*But the second evolved effect is far, far worse. “Soul Anchoring.” Soul-Reaving does not merely destroy a soul. It anchors the soul to a single, eternal moment of absolute suffering and forces it to experience that moment endlessly, recursively, forever, while simultaneously making the soul completely, utterly indestructible. The Eternal Chorus was not killed. They were not even unmade. Each of the billions of souls was individually, personally, intimately anchored to the exact instant of their inversion, forced to live that single Planck-second of absolute, soul-shredding agony in an infinite, recursive loop for the rest of eternity. They could not escape. They could not die. They could not even go mad, because madness would be a mercy, and Soul-Reaving denies all mercies.*

*The physical manifestation of this was a horror beyond words. Because the Chorus had no physical bodies, Godric’s superpower simply forged bodies for them—not out of matter, but out of their own inverted soul-stuff. The billions of souls were dragged from their abstract, harmonious realm into screaming, bleeding, physical reality, each one forced into a body that was nothing more than a raw, exposed nervous system wrapped in a thin membrane of translucent, agonized flesh. They materialized in the throne room as a vast, writhing, pulsating mountain of flayed, bleeding forms, their mouths open in an eternal, silent scream—silent because their vocal cords had been the first things to tear apart under the strain of their own inverted song. Their eyes, billions of eyes, stared out from that mountain of suffering with an awareness that would never dim, never fade, never end.*

*The collateral damage was, as always, obscene. The inverted soul-frequency did not confine itself to the Chorus. It rippled outward across the psychic plane, a shockwave of pure, weaponized soul-agony that touched every sentient being within a billion light-years. I watched through my scrying matrix as entire civilizations suddenly clutched their heads and began to scream, their own souls resonating with the inverted frequency, their own essences beginning to twist and invert in sympathetic agony. The death toll was not a number. It was a silent, endless scream that echoed across the psychic void. Trillions of trillions of beings had their souls inverted and anchored before Godric even glanced at the collateral damage.*

*He glanced. He observed the spreading wave of soul-death with the mild, detached expression of a man noticing a smudge on a windowpane. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly, reached out with His will and restored every single affected soul in exactly zero seconds. The screaming civilizations were healed, their souls un-inverted, their anchored agonies annulled. The decillions of beings would never know that their immortal essences had almost been torn apart and looped into eternal suffering. The restoration was as casual as a man wiping dust from a shelf.*

*He did not restore the Eternal Chorus. The mountain of flayed, silently screaming souls remains in the throne room, a new addition to the decor. His Majesty has expressed appreciation for the “aesthetic contrast” it provides against the obsidian walls. I walk past it every day. Their eyes follow me. They will follow me forever.*

*I am finished writing. My blood has run dry. I will go now to the healers, and I will ask them to remove my memory of this event. They will tell me it is impossible. They will be right.*

---

My own soul felt cold. Colder than the neutronium pressing down on the vault. Colder than the lightless void of the omniverse’s edge. The Eternal Chorus, beings of pure, immortal soul, had been turned into a permanent, screaming monument of inverted agony because Godric had allowed the tiniest fraction of a single superpower to brush against them. And the superpower had evolved. The Weakness Paradox, that cruel, cosmic mechanism, had ensured that every arrogant thought against Him had only made His abilities more devastating, more unfair, more eternal.

I could feel my hands shaking again as I pushed Korvan’s journal aside. The violet mana in my veins was barely a whisper now, a cowed, terrified thing that wanted to hide from the words I had read. But there were two more journals. Two more horrors.

I reached for the second with fingers that were stiff and cold as a corpse’s.

---

**Year 350,000 — The Incident of the Superpower: “Temporal Oblivion”**
**Penned by High Sorcerer Orlac, 122nd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account in the immediate aftermath of the event, while my temporal senses are still screaming. The chrono-receptors that my thousandfold amplification has cultivated over centuries of arcane study have been burned out entirely, the neural pathways cauterized by sympathetic overload. I will never perceive the flow of time again. I will live the rest of my mortal life trapped in a single, eternal present, unable to sense the passage of moments, the drift of hours, the crawl of years. I do not know if this is a curse or a mercy. I suspect it is both.*

*The opposition was the Temporum Aeternum, a civilization of chrono-gods who had achieved absolute mastery over the fifth dimension. They were not time travelers. They were time itself, having dissolved their individual consciousnesses into the fundamental temporal fabric of the omniverse, becoming the very medium through which cause and effect propagated. They existed simultaneously at every moment of cosmic history, from the birth of the first universe to the heat death of the last, an eternal, unchanging presence that had observed all of existence with the detached serenity of beings who could never be threatened because they were, in a very real sense, the threat. They could erase any being from the timeline simply by deciding that the being’s birth had never occurred. They could loop any civilization into an eternal recursion of its own worst moment. They could accelerate a star’s lifespan to ash in a Planck instant. They were, by any rational measure, absolute.*

*When they learned of Godric, they did not fear Him. They were curious. They observed His timeline—or attempted to. The moment they tried to perceive His existence across the temporal axis, they encountered a void. Not a gap, not a barrier, but a perfect, absolute absence where His temporal signature should have been. This was His absolute concealment, shielding even His temporal existence from external observation. The Temporum Aeternum, intrigued and mildly offended, decided to force the issue. They would reach into the timestream, locate the exact moment of the Stasis Event, and simply prevent it from ever occurring. They would unmake Godric before He ever became Godric.*

*They mobilized their entire civilization—a collective consciousness spanning the entirety of cosmic history—and converged on the Year -10,000. They did not send assassins. They simply rewrote the temporal coordinates of that era, attempting to surgically excise the Stasis Event from the timeline while leaving the rest of history intact. Their surgical strike was a masterpiece of chrono-engineering, a maneuver so precise that it should have altered a single moment without disturbing the causal integrity of the subsequent ten thousand years.*

*Godric was sitting on the obsidian throne. He had been there for one hundred and twelve days. He was completely motionless, requiring nothing, simply existing. He observed the Temporum Aeternum’s temporal surgery with the mild, detached interest of a man watching a spider attempt to weave a web in a hurricane. He did not stir. He did not activate His magic. He did not summon His auras or deploy His shields. He completely suppressed every single one of His other abilities, reducing His active engagement to zero. He allowed only a single superpower to manifest. Temporal Oblivion. But this was not the Temporal Oblivion He had originally acquired. The Weakness Paradox had seen to that.*

*Originally, Temporal Oblivion was a superpower that could erase a being or object from the timeline, removing every trace of its existence across all moments of cosmic history. The Temporum Aeternum believed they were immune because they were the timeline itself. They were catastrophically, laughably wrong.*

*The first evolved effect of Temporal Oblivion is “Causal Fracture.” It does not merely erase a target from time. It fractures the target’s entire causal chain, retroactively unmaking not just the target itself, but every single event that ever touched the target, every consequence that ever rippled outward from its existence, every life that was ever lived because of its influence. When Godric allowed the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of this superpower to manifest, the Temporum Aeternum’s entire causal architecture simply shattered. The chrono-gods, who existed across all of cosmic history, found themselves being unmade not just from a single moment, but from every moment, simultaneously. Their presence in the birth of stars, the formation of galaxies, the evolution of life—all of it was violently, excruciatingly erased, the causal chains snapping like overtensioned strings in a cosmic instrument.*

*But the second evolved effect is an abomination of unfairness. “Temporal Loop Imprisonment.” Temporal Oblivion does not merely erase. It can trap a target in a closed, recursive loop of a single moment, forcing it to experience that moment endlessly while simultaneously existing in a state of permanent, unchanging erasure. The Temporum Aeternum was not simply deleted. They were isolated into a single, endless Planck-instant of their own attempted surgery—the exact moment they reached for the Stasis Event—and forced to loop that moment forever. In that instant, they reach for Godric’s past. In that instant, they feel the Causal Fracture begin to tear them apart. In that instant, they experience the absolute, soul-shattering agony of their entire civilization being unmade. And then the instant restarts. And they reach again. And they are torn apart again. Forever. An eternal recursion of reaching and dying and reaching and dying, their consciousnesses fully intact, fully aware, fully screaming.*

*The collateral damage was, as the journals before me have documented, obscene beyond measurement. The Causal Fracture did not confine itself neatly to the Temporum Aeternum. The shattered causal chains propagated across the temporal fabric, unraveling threads of history across billions of years. I watched through my scrying matrix as entire galactic civilizations simply flickered out of existence, their causal foundations erased, their histories unwritten. And then the Temporal Loop Imprisonment bled, its recursive nature infecting nearby timestreams, trapping countless innocent beings in endless loops of their own worst moments—a child forever falling, a mother forever screaming, a world forever ending. The omniversal timeline convulsed and nearly tore itself apart, a shrieking, chaotic wound of fractured causality that threatened to unmake the very concept of linear existence.*

*Godric observed the temporal collapse with the calm detachment of a man watching a minor traffic accident from a high window. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly, reached into the screaming temporal wound and restored the entire omniversal timeline in exactly zero seconds. Every fractured causal chain was re-woven. Every erased civilization was re-inscribed into history. Every looped innocent was freed from their recursion, their memories gently cleansed of the endless, repeating agony. The restoration was as casual as a man rewriting a single misspelled word in a vast, infinite library.*

*He did not restore the Temporum Aeternum. They remain locked in their eternal Planck-instant of reaching and dying, their silent, endless scream echoing faintly through the temporal background noise of the throne room. If you attune your chrono-receptors to the correct frequency, you can hear them—a continuous, looping shriek of absolute, unending despair. His Majesty sometimes hums along. He says it helps Him think.*

*My chrono-receptors are gone. I will never hear them again. I will never hear time again. I will deliver this journal to the vault and then I will go to the healers, and I will ask them if there is a way to remove the memory of that hum. They will tell me no. They will be right.*

---

The obsidian floor beneath my feet had cracked again. I could feel the hairline fractures radiating outward from my chair, the stone protesting the erratic pulse of my horrified mana. The Temporum Aeternum, beings who were time itself, trapped forever in a single Planck-instant of their own attempted attack, their eternal scream a background hum that the King found musically pleasing. And the Weakness Paradox had made it so. Every arrogant thought against Him had only sharpened this superpower, layered it with new, more terrible effects, turned a weapon of erasure into an instrument of infinite, recursive torture.

I could not breathe. I forced myself to inhale, the cold, metallic air of the vault scraping against my throat like a handful of rusted nails. One journal remained. I reached for it with fingers that were no longer connected to my brain, my body moving on pure, horrified autopilot.

---

**Year 700,000 — The Incident of the Superpower: “Conceptual Petrification”**
**Penned by Lord-Mage Solen, 235th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account with a hand that is perfectly steady, for the simple reason that my nervous system has exceeded its capacity for terror and has simply shut down. I feel nothing. Not the cold of the vault, not the ache of my bones, not the thudding of my heart. I am a hollow shell, a void shaped like a man, and the only thing that fills that void is the image of what I witnessed. The image of the Formless Host. The image of what the King’s superpower did to them. The image of what it continues to do to them, eternally, in the corner of the throne room.*

*The opposition was the Formless Host, a collective of shape-shifting entities who had transcended the very concept of physical form. They were not made of matter, or energy, or even abstract information. They were composed of pure, undifferentiated potential—the raw, primal stuff of existence before it condenses into any specific state of being. They could become anything, adapt to anything, survive anything, because they were, fundamentally, nothing. They had no fixed properties to attack, no defined boundaries to breach, no stable existence to erase. They had drifted through the omniverse for eight billion years, completely untouchable, observing the rise and fall of civilizations with the detached amusement of beings who could never be threatened because they were, in essence, the void between definitions.*

*When Thronegate’s envoys arrived, the Formless Host did not even acknowledge them. They simply expanded their formlessness outward, absorbing the envoys into their undifferentiated mass. Our diplomats were not killed. They were simply unmade—their physical forms dissolved, their consciousnesses scattered into the sea of pure potential, their individual identities erased so completely that they never had a chance to scream. The Host then turned its formless, infinite attention toward Thronegate. It did not declare war. It simply began to drift toward the capital, a slow, patient tide of undifferentiated possibility that would absorb the entire empire without a single act of violence, because violence requires form, and the Host was beyond form.*

*Godric was sitting on the obsidian throne. He had been there for two hundred and ninety days, a span of time that meant nothing to a being who had existed for seven hundred thousand years. He observed the approaching tide of formlessness with the mild, detached gaze of a man watching a particularly boring cloud drift across the sky. He did not stir. He did not activate His magic. He did not summon His auras or deploy His shields. He completely suppressed every single one of His other abilities, reducing His active engagement to a perfect, absolute zero. He allowed only a single superpower to manifest. Conceptual Petrification. But this was not the Conceptual Petrification He had originally acquired. The Weakness Paradox, that eternal engine of cruel escalation, had layered this superpower with effects so devastatingly unfair that even writing about them makes the foundations of my sanity tremble.*

*Originally, Conceptual Petrification was a superpower that could “freeze” a target in place by turning its fundamental concept into an immobile, immutable state—essentially, turning a being into a living statue of itself. The Formless Host believed they were immune because they had no concept to freeze; they were pure, undifferentiated potential. They were catastrophically, laughably wrong.*

*The first evolved effect of Conceptual Petrification is “Conceptual Forging.” It does not merely freeze a target’s existing concept. It forcibly creates a concept for the target if one does not exist, wrenching the target from its formless state into a defined, physical, agonizingly specific form. When Godric allowed the most infinitesimally, incomprehensibly microscopic fraction of this superpower to manifest, the Formless Host was violently, excruciatingly dragged from their sea of pure potential into screaming, bleeding, physical reality. They were not asked what form they wished to take. They were simply *given* a form—a form that Godric’s superpower selected for them, specifically designed to maximize their capacity for suffering. The formless entities, who had never known pain, were suddenly encased in bodies that were nothing more than dense, calcified masses of raw, exposed nerve endings, their every surface hypersensitive to the slightest stimulus, their every movement a symphony of unendurable agony.*

*But the second evolved effect is a horror that even I, after centuries of service, cannot fully process. “Conceptual Locking.” Once a target’s concept has been forged and petrified, it is locked into a state of permanent, unchanging stasis, but with full sensory and cognitive awareness. They cannot move. They cannot change. They cannot adapt. They cannot die. They are fixed forever in the exact state of maximum suffering that the Conceptual Forging created, their minds completely conscious, completely aware, completely trapped. The Formless Host, who had existed for eight billion years as pure, free-floating potential, was reduced to a single, stationary, impossibly dense monument of calcified nerve-flesh, their countless consciousnesses packed together in a screaming, immobile mass, each one experiencing the absolute maximum possible pain for the rest of eternity.*

*But there is a third evolved effect. The deepest cruelty. “Conceptual Propagation.” The petrification does not stop with the original target. It propagates outward along the target’s conceptual connections—any being, any entity, any idea that shared any conceptual resonance with the target. The Formless Host, being pure potential, had touched everything. Their conceptual resonance was woven into the very fabric of the omniverse. The propagation wave exploded outward, touching countless trillions of beings who had never even heard of the Host, simply because their existence shared some abstract, philosophical resonance with the concept of formlessness. I watched through my scrying matrix as entire civilizations suddenly froze in mid-motion, their bodies petrifying into screaming, immobile statues of calcified flesh, their minds trapped in eternal, conscious stasis. The propagation did not stop. It spread across the omniversal fabric, touching dimensions, realities, planes of existence—anywhere that the abstract concept of “potential” held meaning. The petrification wave threatened to freeze all of creation into a single, eternal, screaming monument of itself.*

*Godric observed the spreading wave of petrification with the mild, detached gaze of a man watching a ripple spread across a pond. He did not frown. He did not sigh. He simply, effortlessly, instantly, reached into the petrification wave and halted it in exactly zero seconds.
