He restored every frozen civilization, healed every petrified being, unlocked every trapped consciousness. The restored beings would never know they had almost become eternal, screaming statues. The restoration was as casual as a man pausing a song mid-play and then resuming it without missing a note.*

*He did not restore the Formless Host. They remain in the corner of the throne room, a dense, calcified monument of screaming, immobilized nerve-flesh, their countless consciousnesses locked in eternal, unending agony. His Majesty has taken to calling it “the Conversation Piece.” He occasionally taps it with His finger when He is thinking, enjoying the faint, crystalline ringing sound it produces—a sound that is, I have come to understand, the combined, harmonic vibration of their eternal screams. The sound is quite beautiful, in a horrifying sort of way. I have grown used to it. I suspect that is not a good sign.*

*I will deliver this journal to the vault. I will walk past the Conversation Piece on my way out. I will not look at it. I will never look at it again. But I will hear its ringing in my dreams for the rest of my mortal life, and I will know, with absolute, unshakeable certainty, that the same fate awaits anyone, anywhere, who dares to oppose the eternal throne.*

---

I closed Solen’s journal with hands that were no longer shaking. The trembling had stopped. The erratic pulsing of my violet mana had ceased. I was calm. I was hollow. I was a perfect, crystalline void, and the only thing that filled that void was the image of the Conversation Piece—the eternal, screaming monument of calcified nerve-flesh that sat in the corner of the throne room I had just been sitting in, sipping jasmine tea, while Godric smiled warmly and asked about my studies.

I had not noticed it. It had simply been part of the decor, an abstract, crystalline sculpture that caught the amber light of the arcane sconces in a pleasing, shimmering pattern. I had thought it was art. I had thought it was beautiful.

Now I knew. Now I knew that the faint, musical ringing I had heard in the background of my conversation with the King was the eternal, harmonic scream of an entire civilization of beings who had been dragged from pure potential into everlasting, immobile agony. And Godric had tapped His finger against it, absently, while asking me if I preferred jasmine or chamomile.

The horror was so absolute, so total, that my mind simply stopped processing it. A protective wall slammed down around the core of my consciousness, sealing the terror behind a thick, insulating barrier of clinical detachment. I could feel it there, the screaming, clawing thing in the back of my skull, but I could not hear it. Not yet. The walls would crumble eventually—I knew this from the journals, from the trembling hands of my father, from the hollow eyes of every court wizard who had ever served. But for now, in this single, suspended moment, I was calm.

I rose from the obsidian chair on legs that felt like they belonged to someone else. I gathered the three journals—Korvan’s, Orlac’s, Solen’s—and placed them carefully on the shelf beside the five I had already read. Eight journals. Eight ancestors. Eight impossible, horrifying demonstrations of the King’s absolute, unchallengeable, infinitely escalating supremacy.

And these were only eight. The shelves of the vault stretched into the infinite darkness, lined with thousands more. Thousands of accounts. Thousands of massacres. Thousands of superpowers, each one more devastating than the last, each one evolved by the Weakness Paradox into a weapon of cruelty so absolute that the concept of mercy had long since become a forgotten, irrelevant joke.

I climbed the spiral staircase on legs that were not my own, my hand trailing against the cold, sweating stone of the walls. The amber light grew brighter as I ascended, the distant chiming of the mirror shards and the crystalline ringing of the Conversation Piece filtering down through the layers of ancient rock. By the time I reached the top of the stair, I could hear the wet, gurgling moan of the Scentless Order’s eternal death-rattle, the soft, discordant hum of the Chrono-Reapers’ stretched screams, the faint, harmonious vibration of the Calicified Host’s petrified agony.

The throne room was a symphony of eternal suffering.

Godric was waiting for me when I pushed open the heavy golden doors. He was sitting on the obsidian throne, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the soft amber glow of the sconces, His short salt-and-pepper hair neat and tidy, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments. He looked exactly as He always had. Warm. Kind. Paternal. The absolute concealment was flawless, hiding the infinite, recursive, multiplying abyss of His power behind a simple, friendly, forty-five-year-old face.

He looked up as I entered, and His eyes crinkled at the corners. His smile was genuine, warm, filled with a love that I knew, with absolute certainty, was entirely real.

“Kaedan,” He said, His rich baritone vibrating through the vast, screaming-silent chamber. “You have read the additional journals. I can see it in your eyes. The hollow calm. The protective dissociation.” He tilted His head slightly, His expression flickering with a gentle, almost paternal concern. “Are you alright, my boy?”

I looked at Him—at the being who had casually, effortlessly annihilated the Eternal Chorus with soul-inverting agony, who had trapped the Temporum Aeternum in an eternal loop of reaching and dying, who had turned the Formless Host into a beautiful, crystalline monument of petrified suffering. I looked at the Conversation Piece, shimmering softly in the corner. I looked at the mountain of gore that moaned eternally at the foot of the dais. I looked at the billion screaming mirror shards, chiming their endless, crystalline screams.

And then I looked back at my King, my Uncle Godric, the warm, kind, paternal being who had loved my bloodline for a million years and would love it for a million more.

“I am fine, Uncle Godric,” I said, my voice steady and calm. “The journals were… educational.”

He smiled again, that simple, friendly, fatherly smile, and gestured to the small, ornate chair at His right hand. “Good. Come, sit. The jasmine tea is still warm. And you have not yet told me about your progress with the spatial-compression wards you were developing.”

I walked up the steps of the dais, past the mountain of gore, past the Screaming Mirrors, past the Conversation Piece, and took my place at the right hand of the apex predator of all creation. I accepted the cup of jasmine tea He offered me, and I took a slow, careful sip.

The tea was warm. The dread was cold. And the love was real.

“Yes, Uncle Godric,” I said, settling into my chair. “The wards are progressing well. I believe I have found a way to compress a three-dimensional binding array into a two-dimensional runic matrix without losing conceptual integrity…”

Godric listened with genuine, paternal interest, His eyes bright with the simple joy of hearing His beloved court wizard talk about arcane theory. And as I spoke, the eternal, beautiful, horrifying music of the throne room played its endless accompaniment—the chime of the mirrors, the moan of the gore, the ring of the calcified Host, the hum of the looped chrono-gods, the drifting, fading whisper of vaporized Colossi.

This was my life now. This was my service. I was the thirty-fourth court wizard of Blake’s lineage, the newest guardian of a King who needed no guardian, the newest witness to an eternity of absolute, unchallengeable, mercifully cruel supremacy.

And I would serve Him faithfully, loyally, and with unshakeable love, for all the remaining days of my mortal life.

Because opposing Him was not merely suicide. It was an invitation to an eternity of suffering so absolute, so creatively agonizing, so infinitely recursive, that the very concept of escape had been retroactively erased from the omniverse.

The tea was very good. I told Him so. He smiled, pleased, and refilled my cup.
